In Turncoat, an "identity deck" consisting of 10s and a Joker is dealt out to the players, with the player getting the Joker being the secret "turncoat".  A game consists of several missions.  On your turn you look at the top two cards of the deck and select one for the mission; all players then play a face down card and add one from the deck before shuffling and revealing them; cards that match the suit of the mission card add to the total while others subtract from it.  On a turn a player can accuse another of being   a turncoat, but you must be right to win.  Otherwise the game is won by the team if they succeed in 5 missions, and won by the turncoat if the team loses 5 missions.

—user summary

Some of the most prolific designs of the last couple of decades come from teams of designers. Bruno Faidutti, a prolific French author, and Alan R Moon (who appears again later in this book) paired up in 2005 to create a game called Diamant, which later appeared as Incan Gold. The genre of this game is generally referred to as 'Push your Luck', wherein luck is mitigated by players' greed for more. Not unlike the television show Deal or No Deal, where players try for more and more cash by opening cases, some for good, some for bad, Incan Gold sees players flipping cards to gather treasure with some being traps. Our version, Break In, stays true to the formula, as players abseil into a bank, getting their mitts on as much dough as they can before alerting the guards.

—description from the designer

This game is part of Femtitva, a collection of 10 games by Scott Huntington that were inspired by popular modern games but can be played with a traditional deck of cards.

In Break In, a deck with 7-10s removed is dealt into 3 equal stacks (4 for 4 players), and each player gets to look at one stack before it's shuffled and placed face down (this represents "Scouting the bank").  Players take turns flipping a top card from any stack, which is either a guard (picture card) or cash (non-picture card).  Two guards of the same suit end a round, with players still in the bank at that point gaining no cash; you only keep cash if you leave the bank on your turn, in which case you'll score points for things such as the pip values of cards in front of you.  The player with most cash after 5 rounds is the winner.

—user summary

The game Ticket to Ride, by aforementioned designer Alan R Moon, is known by many gamers, somewhat darkly, as a 'gateway game'. It is easy to grasp, has a simple rules set, and an uncomplicated turn structure. Indeed, it is one of the first modern games I played, and it was the game that started my ever-ballooning collection. It involves collecting sets of cards to spend on creating train routes along a colourful board. It has been showered with various accolades, and rightly so. Black Snake takes the sturdy frame of Ticket to Ride and boils it down to a 52-card affair. Players are still laying track, albeit in a more abstract setting.

—description from the designer

This game is part of Femtitva, a collection of 10 games by Scott Huntington that were inspired by popular modern games but can be played with a traditional deck of cards.

In Black Snake, players start with a hand of three cards while three cards are face up next to the draw pile and are available for drawing, while cards equal to the number of players are played face-up as the starting "Track".  On your turn you either: 1. draw two cards (face-up ones or from the face-down pile); or: 2. lay track, by playing an ascending or descending sequence (ignoring suit) on top of one of the current "Tracks".  These earn points depending on the size of the sequence (1 card = 1 point, 2 cards = 3 points, 3 cards = 5 points, 4+ cards = 7 points).  The first player to reach 30 points (or 40 points for a longer game) is the winner.

—user summary

The genre of abstract strategy games is undoubtedly the one with the richest history. Ancient sets of Chess, Checkers, Go, Shogi, and Mancala are still being discovered by archaeologists, simultaneously making it hard to pinpoint a date of creation, and proving board games' place of importance through the ages. Abstracts tend to have a very hard- core set of followers: players will devote their entire lives to one game, and in parts of Eastern Europe, Chess players are celebrities, scoring lucrative advertising deals and being followed by the paparazzi. New abstracts however, such as Hey, That's My Fish, by Finno-German team Alvydas Jakeliunas and Günter Cornett, have also found a place among the heavyweights of abstract games. Jackhammer is a fairly faithful analogue of that game, but employs a Dr Reiner Knizia-like scoring system (more on the Doctor later).

—description from the designer

This game is part of Femtitva, a collection of 10 games by Scott Huntington that were inspired by popular modern games but can be played with a traditional deck of cards.

In Jackhammer, a deck of Aces through 9s is dealt into a 6x6 face-up grid, and each player puts their "worker" (a unique picture card) on a different Ace.  On their turn player moves his worker in any straight line (orthogonally or diagonally) as far as they wish onto a free card, and they take the card they started from (creating a hole in the layout) and put it in front of them.  Movement may not pass over a hole or another worker; if they can't move they take the card they're on and have no more turns.  Players earn points for the pip values of the cards they've taken, and the highest pip value total for the suit they've scored the lowest points for determines the winner.

—user summary

The grandfather of modern gaming is no doubt the late Sid Sackson (1920-2002). He was a prolific game author, collector, and crafted some designs that were decades ahead of their time. If there is a prevalent board game still in the market, then there is also a Sackson game which strives to surpass it: Acquire is his much less luck-driven take on Monopoly, Buy Word is a Scrabble-esque game that rewards creative anagramming instead of knowing all the two-letter words, and Sleuth is his (in my opinion, clearly superior version of) Clue (or Cluedo, if you're Australian like me). It is a pure logic puzzle, where players try to solve the mystery of the stolen jewels. Our little whodunit, Time of Death, is of a similar vein, but players each have their own mystery to solve.

—description from the designer

This game is part of Femtitva, a collection of 10 games by Scott Huntington that were inspired by popular modern games but can be played with a traditional deck of cards.

In T.O.D. (Time Of Death), a 24 card deck of Aces through 6s is used, sorted into red cards (motives: Hearts = Love, Diamonds = Greed) and black cards (weapons: Spades = Bat, Clubs = Shovel).  Players each get one random card from each pile, which is what the player on their right is trying to guess.  The Time of Death (TOD) that must be guessed is calculated as the sum of the two numbers (hours) and the lower number followed by the higher number (minutes).  Four face-up cards are on the table, and place the top random card from the deck onto one of these four cards, then ask the player on your left a question about these four cards: "Is a number there?" "Is a number missing?" "Is a suit there?" "Is a suit missing?", with just a yes/no answer given.  By logic you try to deduce the solution, and may guess the answer on your turn instead of asking a question; if you're the first player to get the correct answer, you win the game.

—user summary

One thing that certain modern board games have a knack at is redirecting eye focus from the table to each other. Ca$h 'N Gun$, by Ludovic Maublanc, is one such game. Set in what seems like a Quentin Tarantino film, Ca$h 'N Gun$ sees rival players pointing foam pistols at each other in a Mexican stand-off. It is not so much a game about the pieces (although the pistols are a nice touch) but it is about the players, and being able to read them. In a vein, it shares similarities with Rock Paper Scissors, which is also a simultaneous guessing game, albeit with more nuanced bluffing and goals. Virus also attempts to lift the gamers' heads, in order to pass some (proverbial) germs around.

—description from the designer

This game is part of Femtitva, a collection of 10 games by Scott Huntington that were inspired by popular modern games but can be played with a traditional deck of cards.

Virus uses a Joker plus a number of other cards depending on the players, with suits/values being ignored: 2 players: 8 red, 9 black; 3 players: 12 red, 11 black; 4 players: 12 red, 15 black.  Each player starts with an equal number of red cards (Medicine), while the Joker (Virus) is shuffled with the black cards (Germs), from which players are dealt an equal hand.  On the count of three players simultaneously make a symbol corresponding to one of the following actions: Drink medicine (clenched fist), Infect player (point one finger at that person), Steal from player (point two fingers at that person), Block player from targeting you (point hand at that person).  Drinking medicine makes you turn a red card face down, Infected players draw a card from the person that targeted them, Stealing medicine lets you steal a face-up red card from the targeted person; Blocking protects you against people pointing at you.  In a 3-4 player game, at the end  of a round (all medicine besides two are drunk) players score for medicine (+4) and germs (-1), or get just 1 point if they have the Virus; the player with most points after three rounds is the winner.  In a 2 player game, you win a round if you're first to drink four potions and don't have the virus; the first player to win two rounds is the winner.

—user summary

The biggest celebrity in the modern board gaming word is arguably Dr Reiner Knizia. With over 500 published games and a cupboard full of awards, the doctor is known for his simple ideas that contain hidden depth. It does not come as a surprise, after playing various designs of his, that he earned his PhD in the field of mathematics. His titles' mechanics have a mathematical precision to them; so much so that 'kniziaesque' is entering the vernacular of many an aficionado. Samurai, one of his most highly praised titles, is about clever placement of pawns on a map of Japan. In Paparazzi, we are trying to snap the best shots of those famous picture cards while they are on the red carpet.

—description from the designer

This game is part of Femtitva, a collection of 10 games by Scott Huntington that were inspired by popular modern games but can be played with a traditional deck of cards.

In Paparazzi, the 12 court cards ("Celebs") are laid out in a 4x3 grid with space for one card between each row and column.  Players get cards from a single suit (2 players: Ace-10; 3 players: 2-10; 4 players: 3-10), and draw 3 cards from their own shuffled deck.  In turns players put one of their hand cards in a free space orthogonally adjacent to one or two Celebs and draw a new hand card.  When the last card is laid, each Celeb is given to the player with the highest pip total for their cards beside it.  The winner is the player with the most points, with these Celebs scored: King = 5, Queen = 4, Jack = 3, and two or three Celebs of the same suit gets a 2 or 3 point bonus.

—user summary

I hope that I have so far demonstrated that there are many possibilities to be had with a humble deck of 52 cards (the name of this booklet by the way means 52 in Swedish, a language I sporadically try to learn). Be it creative ways to use cards, or interesting timing mechanisms, there are infinite ways to put a deck to use. Some people even hone their dexterity with cards; throwing them with stunning accuracy tens of metres into watermelons, or building houses with a steady hand. While this last game is not a dexterity game and doesn't really borrow heavily from another game, it could be compared to Flowerfall, by Carl Chudyk, where players are trying to drop cards from a height onto others. There is no dropping in Hatchet, but burying other symbols is key.

—description from the designer

This game is part of Femtitva, a collection of 10 games by Scott Huntington that were inspired by popular modern games but can be played with a traditional deck of cards.

In Hatchet, you start by turning the top card face-up from a face-down shuffled deck.  Players take turns drawing a card and placing it so it covers face-up pips, with a maximum of one pip for each suit, and only if the entire pip is covered.  Points are scored for the number of pips covered (i.e. a maximum of four per turn).  Picture cards are not placed but just earn 2 points when turned up.  The game ends when the 10th picture card is turned up, and at that point the player with most points is the winner.

—user summary

boardgame: Valheim: The Board Game – Bauernbrot / Flammekueche Promo Card
description: Valheim recipe card for Bauernbrot / Flammekueche. Spiel 2025 promo card.

Area 636: Pursuit of A.E.G.I.S. is a tactical adventure game set in a secret military research facility deep in the American desert. The world's powers are racing to infiltrate "Area 636" and steal fragments of a revolutionary technology known as A.E.G.I.S. — an artificial intelligence defense system that can predict and neutralize threats before they occur. Each player assumes the role of a special agent representing their nation's interests with distinct motivations and abilities that shape how they approach infiltration, sabotage, and survival.

Gameplay unfolds on a modular 7x7 map, representing the labyrinthine base filled with traps, secret passages, laser grids, and heavily armed security. Players explore, uncover tiles, gather data fragments, and face off against both automated defenses and rival agents. Each agent has unique powers and limited resources, and must balance risk and stealth — every misstep raises the alarm tracker, bringing the entire base closer to lockdown. Combat relies on dice rolls, tactical bonuses, and the clever use of ability cooldowns.

The goal is to be the first to collect enough A.E.G.I.S. data fragments and escape alive. In multiplayer, players can fight or sabotage each other. In solo mode, one agent must survive alone against the facility's defenses.

Dungeon Pages: Underboss is a standalone, print and play adventure that brings new layers of tension, strategy, and replayability to the tabletop. Underboss deepens the series’ core ideas — simple mechanics, smart decisions, and tight design — while introducing fresh twists. It’s for fans who love the compact strategy of Dungeon Pages, but want to explore new tactical challenges from the perspective of the evil underlord. This time, instead of raiding the dungeon and defeating monsters, you'll build traps and deploy monsters to protect your domain. You'll even drop into the dungeon yourself to be sure the job is done right.

As the heroes crawl over the catacombs, you must balance building traps, deploying defensive forces, and when to attack with your own unique boss powers. You do this by rolling dice, strategically marking the results as traps, monsters, and powerful effects to defeat heroes, gather soul gems, and build your forces.  The dice also activate the heroes’ positions and attacks that you will have to mitigate. Defeat all the heroes to win!

Each game sheet will feature a unique configuration of familiar heroes. The heroes will try to break down your defenses and run off with your gold. If they do, you'll be defeated. But if you can eliminate the invaders, you'll reign supreme and finally be declared the ultimate Underboss!

—description from the publisher

Treasure Trails: Irate Pirate’s Edition is a turn-based 2 player  adventure game where rival sailors race to uncover the lost riches of the infamous Irate Pirate. Legend tells of his vast fortune — half buried on an island and half sunk at sea — waiting to be found by daring explorers.

Players start in their home villages, exploring nearby land tiles to reveal terrain, resources, and clues. Each turn follows three phases — Exploration, Movement, and Resource Collection — as players uncover new areas, move strategically, and gather items to progress in a game that feels like a classic Graphic Adventure.

As resources are collected, players can acquire ships to sail across ocean tiles, expanding their search for treasure. Action Cards add twists, letting players alter turns, gain advantage, or sabotage rivals along the way, keeping the fun until the last turn.

The goal is to be the first player to reach a treasure tile with the required resources to claim it and win the game.

This box contains everything you need to enhance your Vampire Lords gaming experience:

- A new asymmetrical faction of vampire hunters with three types of hunters
- The ability to expand the game to 6 players and two additional vampire lords
- A scenario book
- Event cards

boardgame: Wispwood: Alternate player cats & Scoring Promo Card
description:
Alternate set of 6 player cats with different art + an extra scoring card (for the orbs) for Wispwood.

It was given for free if you pre-ordered the game from CGE and will be for sale later.

Balloon Boing is a fast-paced, abstract strategy game for two players, where rival teams of Cat and Dog balloonists compete for supremacy over the cloud arena.

The goal is to either eliminate the opponent's pieces through skillful maneuvers or conquer both of their crucial Home Base spaces.

The core of the game is the Boing Squad: Single balloons are weak, but when they link up in a line, they form a mighty unit. Only a numerically superior Boing Squad can push the opposing balloons from their positions—or send them flying right off the board!

Watch out for the Tornados: Every time you lose a balloon, you get the consolation prize of placing a chaotic Tornado Marker on the board. Push a balloon into a Tornado—or the Tornado onto a balloon—and both pieces are immediately removed from the game!

Master the teamwork of your Boing Squad and utilize the turbulent whirlwinds to dominate the cloud arena!

-description from designer
Song of Silveranth: Frost Ballad is an expansion for Song of Silveranth. Frost Ballad contains: 32 Page Story Book, 16 Page Location Book, 90 New Cards, and 30 New Enemies.

In Critter Miners, players take command of their animal team and complete to control spaces on a hex-shaped mine board.
During each turn, Players place their token to surround tiles. when an area is fully enclosed, the player with the highest combined power gain the resource token from that area.

—description form the publisher

Overview

Gods, Warriors & Beasts is not just a board game — it is a mirror of life itself.

Every player begins as a Beast, driven by instinct and desire, walking the path of experience through trials, virtue, and fate. Along the way, one may ascend to the state of the Warrior — mastering self-discipline and courage — and ultimately, for the few who understand the code, awaken as the God within.

The board represents the journey of the soul. Two paths unfold: one leading toward material gain, the other toward inner purification. Coins reward worldly triumphs, while wisdom removes the vices that bind.

At the heart of the game lies Tile 42: The Philosopher’s Code, where the final sphinx style riddle is revealed — a question that leads to the ultimate glory.

Inspired by ancient philosophy, sacred geometry, and the mysteries of enlightenment, Gods, Warriors & Beasts invites players to see play as ritual, and strategy as reflection. It was not designed to be mass-produced, but preserved as a symbol — a spark for those ready to remember.

— “Life is the board. You are the player.”

⸻

Setup

Each player begins as a Beast, receiving seven Vice Bands (representing the seven deadly sins) and a small number of Coins.
Tokens are placed at the starting point of the spiral path known as The Tungam.

⸻

Turn Sequence

On their turn, a player:
    1.    Rolls the die and moves their token forward.
    2.    Draws a card based on the color or symbol of the space landed on, these may include:
    •    Knowledge Cards — general or historical trivia that earn Coins.
    •    Philosophy & Mythology Cards – mythological or philosophical questions that, if answered correctly, remove a Vice Band and earn chi stones.
    •    Natal Cards – symbolic tales connecting to the game’s lore.
    •    Philosopher’s Code Cards – rare riddle questions testing higher understanding.
    3.    Acts on the result – gaining Coins, losing Vices, or facing special trials depending on the card’s outcome.

⸻

Paths to Victory
    •    Material Path: Players collect Coins, seeking wealth and dominance.
    •    Virtuous Path: Players shed all Vice Bands, achieving spiritual balance.

A player may choose either path but cannot master both. The deeper they go into one, the more difficult it becomes to cross into the other.

⸻

End of the Game

When a player reaches Tile 42 – The Weighing of the Heart, they face the final Philosopher’s Code Question if they have choosen to remove their vices during the game.
A correct answer represents enlightenment, the Mortal ascends, transforming into the God Figure. 
Players who have choosen to play for coins at this point count up their coins and claim victory, but at this point they reached as far as they can go, they cannot play the riddle and cannot change from the beast figure.
The game ends here and victory is determined by what the individual players deem as a personal victory — material or spiritual wealth.

Players can opt to play another lap 84 tiles if they wish.

⸻

Theme

Gods, Warriors & Beasts invites players to reflect on their own nature through play, testing wisdom, willpower, and moral insight.
It is both a game of choices and a living allegory, revealing two ways to play life itself:
the pursuit of gold or the pursuit of light.

—description from the publisher

Pelagos Sanctuary is a competitive card-drafting and pattern-building game inspired by the real marine protected area shared by Italy, France, and Monaco.
Each player manages their own marine sanctuary, expanding it turn after turn by playing cards that represent habitats and management strategies.

During each round, players simultaneously choose one card from their hand and decide whether to play it as a Sea card (to expand their marine area and gain resources) or as a Strategy card (to activate conservation actions or end-game objectives). They then pass the remaining cards to the next player, following a closed drafting mechanism.

Sea cards provide food resources used to attract Marine Mammal cards - whales, dolphins, and seals - while also introducing potential Threat tokens such as fishing, shipping, yachting, or pollution. Strategy cards allow players to mitigate these threats, turn tokens to their safe side, and score additional points through sustainable management.

At the end of each round, an Event card is revealed: if threats are not mitigated, they trigger real consequences like the loss or migration of animals between sanctuaries.
After five rounds, the game ends. Players score points for the number and diversity of marine species in their sanctuary, successful strategies, and special recognition.
The player with the highest total score becomes the most effective guardian of the Pelagos Sanctuary.

Eco-friendly in both theme and production, the game is printed entirely with FSC-certified and biodegradable materials.

—description from the designer

Do you think you can handle this muvuca?

Strong sun, sand on your feet, and the smell of the Atlantic sea... While some go to Brazilian beaches to have fun, others go to try to make a living! You are street vendors taking advantage of the hustle and bustle to earn some money. Who will win over the crowd?

Muvuca is a fast-paced, tactical duel microgame whose objective is to win a two-dimensional tug-of-war. In each turn, you place a new card on the grid, pushing rows and columns to create the largest sets of customers who want to buy identical items. Each move you make helps you, but also hinders your opponent: a tactical, fast-paced, and portable experience.

—description from the designer

Microfishing is a card game for two where players take on the role of contestants in a fishing contest! The catch is that they're trying to catch exactly the right number of very tiny fish.

Over a series of rounds, players will use cards from their hand to capture fish from the Lake that add up to 18. Be careful of The Turtle though, as it will eat one of your captured fish if you catch it!

Microfishing is based on the classic Spanish card game Escoba, but the capture value to aim for in Microfishing is 18.

On their turn, if a player's card adds up to 18 with any of the Lake cards in the middle of the table, they capture them. Each round, the players who capture the most fish, the rarest fish, the prettiest fish, and the Tiniest Fish will earn points toward winning the game.

As with other games in the same family as Escoba and Scopa, if a player captures all the cards from the middle of the table, they earn a bonus point: a Bag!

The game is also completely playable with a set of double-six dominoes. Each card in Microfishing is just the sum of its corresponding tile.

-description from designer

In Crosswhords! players cooperatively construct a barred crossword, a crossword where heavy lines drawn along grid squares separate words instead of black squares.

Players start with an empty grid. They devise a list of categories and then enter a word or phrase in the middle of the grid.

On your turn, you think of a word that fits in one of the categories and in the grid. If the other players guess your word, you write it down in the grid. If not, you give the other players a clue by writing a related word in the grid.

Everyone wins if there's a word or phrase in the grid for each of the categories.

—description from the designer
family: Mechanism: Give a Clue / Get a Clue

description:
How can you recognise a Polish nobleman?

By his magnificent national attire: the żupan, kontusz, and a sabre at his side. The final touch to the outfit is its most decorative element - the kontush sash, also known as the słucki belt.

A nobleman would never leave his manor without it. He must wrap himself in the belt!

Obwiąż się! is a dynamic board game in which you take on the roles of Polish Armenians - owners of persjarnie (workshops producing exquisite kontush belts).

You will compete for the favour of magnates and nobles who are waiting for belts woven in specific colours and patterns.
To win, you’ll need sharp observation, a good memory, and nerves of steel during negotiations.

The objective of the game is to accumulate the biggest number of points. These will be primarily gathered by selling your kontush belts to magnates and noblemen.

During your turn, you will be able to perform one of the following things:


     Gather income
     Acquire new kontush belt fragments
     Exchange fragments
     Initiate an auction to fulfil the order placed by one of the nobles. 


The player who manages to satisfy the largest number of clients will become the winner.

An enchanting party game packed with outrageous curses, wild challenges, and side-splitting laughter.

You work to attempt to get 5 curse cards in your "curse purse" - and the curses enacted on you impact you in ways that are range from goofy (e.g., having someone pour your drink in your mouth without spilling) to impacting game play. The first to gain 5 curses in their curse purse wins.

boardgame: Modern Armored Warfare: Desert Storm 1991 and Croatia 1995
description:
The Modern Armored Warfare Series is a two-player, operational level wargame of armored warfare in the second half of the 20th century. This set includes two games: Operation Desert Storm (Kuwait and Iraq, 1991) and Operation Storm (Croatia and Bosnia, 1995).

The “Common Rules” described below apply to all games in the series. However, some games may modify these common rules.
Each game has its own “Game-Specific Rules.” If any content conflicts with the Common Rules, the Game-Specific Rules take precedence.

Published in issue #27 of Banzai Magazine, November 2025.

Monstermachy is a fast paced strategy game centered around building a personalized team of pieces and conquering the landscape to defeat your opponent.

A mastery of terrain traversal, punishing offense, and reading your opponents intentions are all needed to come out on top in this battle of the ages. The goal is to capture your opponent’s last remaining Nest while keeping their monster units at bay. A unique aspect of Monstermachy is being able to bring important pieces you have lost back to the board by Hatching them from your remaining Nests. Do you sacrifice your precious Nests to maintain pressure on the board or do you force your opponent to spread their offence thin by targeting all of your vitally important Nests? Only you can decide. A well placed or timed monster hatching can turn the entire tide of battle, so never count yourself out of the fight! Take care to familiarize yourself with the unique Traits of both you and your opponent’s monsters. These abilities, while no substitute for good tactics, can alter the outcome of an entire match.

Set forth in fierce competition or just have fun making personally distinct teams and combinations!

—description from the designer

A new type of candy and a party hat add new challenges to Brigadeiro! Additionally, with this expansion, it's possible to play with 5 players.


     The "olho de sogra" candy starts by giving negative points, but can guarantee victory if you get used to the taste (4 cards).
     The party hat will jump from hand to hand until it gives points to someone at the end of the game (1 card).

New people arrive at the Troças to add more challenges to your game!

4 new cards, with new effects and wild card items, will spice up your matches with more possibilities.

"To endure the party and recover from the fatigue, come to Bar do Cabeleira!"

This promotional card for Troças makes the game a little easier, allowing players to leave a reveler resting at the bar instead of placing them in a Troça.

Canadian poker dice game published by Copp Clark in 1978. The goal is similar to Liar's Dice, but only 1 set of dice is rolled and passed around. Each player must determine if the previous player is lying about what they rolled. Last to get caught lying or incorrectly guess a liar 6 times wins
family: Components: Dice Cup / Dice Tower / Dice Tray

description:
Survive working life, don't go crazy with stress, play action cards, and avoid work cards to be the last worker standing. 
The game has 2 different decks (action and work) each turn a player will draw a work card from the work deck, this card will add stress to the player and then the player will choose if use a action card(to reduce stres, add stress to another player or different actions to manage the stress) or draw a new action card from the action deck, once a player gets 10 points of stress (work cards or stress tokens) will be out of the game so the last player without 10 stress points will be the winner.

boardgame: O Canada: Power Politics in the True North, 1950-2030
description:
O Canada is a 1-4 player board game depicting the ebb and flow of power relationships in Canada in the 20th and 21st centuries. Each player takes the role of a faction that represents a general political outlook or school of thought in Canadian politics (not a specific political party, though sometimes a party is closely identified with a particular philosophy(,

Using political and social actions and exploiting various events, players build and maneuver force pieces to influence or control the population and the issues of the day or otherwise achieve their Faction's aims. A deck of cards regulates turn order, events, victory checks, and other processes.

This game uses an adaptation of the COIN system used in various games published by GMT Games. The original system is copyright GMT Games and is used by permission.
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

description:
Sushi Otter is a poker-like game where cards represent sushi.
Each round, players select cards from their hand to form a hand and compete with other players for the highest score. The score is the sum of the hand value, the selected cards themselves, and any bonuses from enhancement cards.
Early on, you won't get strong hands unless you're incredibly lucky. However, by acquiring enhancement cards each round, you'll be able to build unbelievably powerful hands.

—description from the designer
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

description:
Hanging Empire is a game about the political struggles within Japan during the period from the Meiji Restoration to Great War.

The basic system is based on  the French Revolution 1789 from Game Journal issue 89. A unique feature of this game is players struggle against specific enemy powers, such as the former Shogunate, Qing Empire or Russia, which appear each turn, using diplomatic and military power.
Players aim to ensure their faction holds a dominant position in the process of making the Japanese Empire one of the world's great powers.

Each turn, players acquire characters(politicians, military officers, businessmen) and events through an auction, the Prime Minister allocates ministerial posts, take factional actions(voting or production), resolve diplomacy and war against enemy power, victory points are awarded accordingly, and finally, a new Prime Minister is elected based on the voting results.

The winner is determined by Victory Points (VP), which are earned when the government and the characters in faction's  ideology of align each turn, and through the achievements of the key ministers in the cabinet.

This game is scheduled to be published as an insert in Game Journal magazine.

The title 坂の上の帝国(Saka no Ue no Teikoku) means Empire Above the Hill, and is homage to "Saka no Ue no Kumo" (Clouds Above the Hill), a famous historical novel by Ryotaro Shiba depicting the Russo-Japanese War.

boardgame: Forest Shuffle: Dartmoor – River Lyd Promo Card
description:
Moor: River Lyd
DP01 Promo Card

Bonus: do one more action
Scores: 1 point for each dragonfly

Players work together to decorate Gabby’s Dollhouse by revealing and switching around chips on a board. On your turn, roll the die to move the cats forward, swap chips, or reveal items. Revealed chips in the correct spot stay face up.

The game ends as soon as all items are placed face-up in the correct spots or when the Gabby Cats reach the end of the track.If the cats reach the end, check if all items are in their correct spots: If they are, the players still win. If any items are in the wrong place, they lose the game.
family: Theme: Rooms: Interiour Design, Home Decoration, Tidying

description:
Plunder Rumble is a quick-to-learn, fast-paced, and highly social card game for 2–6 players, set in a thrilling world of classic pirate adventure.

Each round, the players set sail to different iconic adventures and work together, shaping temporary alliances during raids. But when it is time to share the plunder, it is every pirate for themselves!

Gather Plunder Cards that give you gold or strong instant abilities. The player with the most gathered gold at the end of the game wins!

Bluff, parley and negotiate. Play Crew cards with unique abilities to seize the advantage, or unleash Plunder cards at the perfect moment to turn the tide.

Set up the game in under two minutes and dive straight into chaotic pirate scheming, unexpected twists, and endless replayability. Whether you honor your word or stab your fellow crewmates in the back, one thing is certain: in Plunder Rumble, fortune favors the bold... and the treacherous.

—description from the designer

boardgame: SaaC: Software Engineering as a Card Game
description:
In SaaC, you're the maestro of mayhem – leading a software team through the glorious chaos of enterprise development. Outthink rival teams by balancing ruthless pragmatism with idealistic best practices – all while oscillating between major incidents and mandatory team-building karaoke.

In each round, there is an event to which all teams react with their skills (their hand cards) as efficiently and effectively as possible. At the end, the team with the most victory points wins. These points are made up of the team's motivation, quality, lowered by their pressure and lost skill cards.

SaaC blends dark humor with darker coffee stains.
It's not just a game – it's a survival simulator.

For risks and side effects read the fr*cking manual.
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

description:
Eternal Nightmare is a fast-paced, horror-themed party board game for 2–6 players that blends trivia, real-time challenges, push-your-luck decisions, and take-that interaction.

Players take on the role of eerie characters trying to escape a surreal nightmare realm. Each turn, players move around the board, face unique encounters, answer horror and film-based trivia questions, earn currency, trigger events, and confront monsters. The objective is to collect sacred talismans and survive long enough to face – and beat – one of the final escape challenges.

Gameplay combines:

Horror & film trivia (450 total questions across all content)

Risk-reward encounters and player confrontation

Physical/dexterity mini-challenges and timed tasks

Plot and event cards that alter the game state

A final real-time challenge (dice challenge, tower stack, or other frantic end-game trial)

The theme draws from classic and modern horror, but the tone is fun and comedic rather than scary. The experience centres on energy, laughter, dramatic moments, and friendly rivalry, making it suitable for horror fans, movie lovers, party gamers, and mixed-experience groups.

The base game includes core trivia, combat, and challenge content, and the expansion adds 150 new horror & film trivia questions, bringing the total to 450 questions.

—description from the designer

When the lights go out, the real game begins!

Load Shedding is a chaotic set-collection and sabotage card game about collecting alternate power sources and keeping your home running during a blackout, all while sabotaging your neighbours’ chances.

Players race to assemble working Power Sets from generators, solar panels, and battery backups while dealing with sneaky sabotage, sudden outages, and a constant ticking clock. Every turn counts. Will you stock up on backup power, pull a clever trick, or drain someone else’s supply?

Easy to learn but full of sneaky moves and hilarious twists, Load Shedding is inspired by the real-life power outages that are all too familiar in Pakistan. It turns the frustration of a power cut into a frantic, laugh-filled battle to stay one step ahead of the dark.

—description from the designer

Its springtime and perennial battle of beavers vs. farmers has begun again. Beavers do what comes natural to them, building a lodge on a stream to start a family. These are both master architects and the ultimate squatters. The farmer needs a flowing stream, which is vital for a productive farm life and does not take kindly to having this menace on his property.

So it begins ... Farmer Miller will take drastic measures to keep those beavers from damming up his stream. He will take incendiary cans and place them on the dam while they are building. Gun in hand, he will shoot at the cans to destroy sections of the dam. The farmer knows that there is only so much materials in the area that the beavers can use and wants to encourage them to relocate.

X-Stream Squatters is a solo three-dimensional, tile placement game where you assume the role of beavers trying to build their lodge on the perfect stream. No other place will do and you get to work building your dream home. The farmer wants to keep them from making a permanent home and blocking up his stream, so he will take desperate measure to keep these unwelcomed squatters from making permanent residence.

—description from the designer

A nostalgic 2 player board game, manufactured and released in Greece in the 1970s.

The player with the highest number on the wheel starts first. Players move toward the ball at the top of the tree according to the number they roll on the wheel. When one player moves up, the other player at the other end of the seesaw is pushed away from the ball. Each time someone lands on a square with a star, spins the wheel one more time. The winner is the player who ends up with an exact roll of the wheel on the square marked with the number 12.

XIL is a turn-based strategy game for at least 2 players that brings the dynamic nature and tactical depth of classic RTS games to the home gaming table. The heart of the game is the 15x15 game mat, where your battlefield unfolds. Here you lead your forces into battle, capture strategically important positions, establish outposts and secure valuable resources. Your units can engage in close and ranged combat and use their type-specific abilities to achieve dominance. Titans—powerful command units, further shape the battlefield with their unique abilities.

In addition to the events on the playing field, each player manages their own resource board. Here you keep track of the accumulated XIL—the most important resource in the game and in the world of Uhltra itself. With XIL, you recruit units, purchase upgrades, place outposts, and use abilities—without XIL, your war machine comes to a standstill. In addition, each faction collects its own special resource through certain game actions. In three stages, you unlock powerful faction-specific abilities that can dramatically change the battlefield through one-time use or permanent improvements.

The base game offers high modularity in setting up its playfield and game goals. Besides classical modes for destroying your enemies capital, hunting titans, or controlling all resources, the rulebook also includes special scenarios for sieges, pre-defined standoff battles, 'tower-defense' and more. The battlefield can also be extended to increase size and/or player numbers by combining additional base game boxes.

—description from the publisher

Word Poppers from TOMY Games is the loopy letter juggling game where fingers scramble to grab letters and make up words before it’s too late. Round by round, players turn over their Word card as the timer starts to tick. On it are three letters to which they must add letter tiles from the game board. Pick and place letters one by one, using the printed letter at the beginning or the end, or anywhere in the middle. Hunt down the letter tiles you need to spell out words of two letters or more. But look out for the timer. When it goes, it sends a shudder through the entire game board, blasting the letters about! It’s time to tally up your totals. You’ll earn a point for each letter tile you used in a word, with bonus points for words of five letters or longer. But lose points for left-over tiles you didn’t use or any printed letter on your card that hasn’t been paired with at least one letter tile. On to the next round: pick a new card, drop all the tiles back on the board and start the timer. The first player to score 25 points is the Word Poppers winner!

—description from the publisher

Sackers know Earth is not getting friendlier. As well as their competitors. So, this is where the hunt begins.


     Personal Contract cards are given to the players. When a player receives a Miss card, the contract becomes available to everyone. It creates new opportunities for competitors to gain SiDo (VPs).
     The Market gets the third row. The Action cards there can be purchased or obtained by completing achievements. These cards will grant players new abilities, aimed at rearranging their Action cards, upgrades, and resources, or fighting Mosses (wild resource tokens) and confronting other players.
     New Area Report cards are available. The resources are now generated to pit players against each other.


—description from the publisher

boardgame: Zombicide: Undead or Alive – E1: The Bank Heist
description:
Two outlaws were looking to make an easy cash and went to small village of Clay Town. Little did they know the new plague would arrive along with them!

OBJECTIVES
• Grab the money and scoot! Reach the Exit with all Survivors.

In Stay Away!, 4-12 players try to track down "The Thing", a creature that has the capability of possessing the human body, cloning it, then taking its place, so as you play you won't know who "The Thing" is or when someone will become "The Thing" or who is infected or not. The researchers are looking for The Thing; their goal is to work together to identify which player is The Thing and roast it with a "Flamethrower" card. You remain Human until The Thing passes an "Infected!" card to you during a card exchange, at which point you become an Infected and take on that new Role: You are now an ally of The Thing and must take care that its identity is not revealed.

What's new in this new version:

All-new graphics and illustrations in pure Escape Studios style.
Minor bug fixes.
Rules improvements.
Introduction of the "Adrenaline" mode that allows you to play more cards during your turn.

New Stay Away cards!

- Grenade!
Throw a grenade that rolls on the table, but be careful 
because after 4 turns it will explode!

- Mirror Reflection!
Avoid being roasted and roast whoever wanted to eliminate you! With this card you will eliminate the player who wanted to eliminate you with a grenade or flamethrower!

- Rage!
If you are the target of an Analysis card, avoid showing your cards and exchange a card with each of the players!

- Dynamite! (Exclusive card for Gamefound pre-order only).
Leave it in front of a teammate, but be careful because whoever is in that place after four turns will explode!

New Panic card effects including the one we are most proud of:

- Feign Death!
The first player who was
eliminated returns to the game in the
role he had before
being eliminated.
Draw 4 “Stay Away!” cards and occupy
the place where you were playing.

What else to say. This is a completely new version that we are sure will give you endless hours of fun, but the real question is another: Can you trust your Friends?

—description from the publisher

Yo ho ho! Pirate Pete from the popular Pop Up Pirate game is on a new adventure! Right now he’s got a lot of weight on his shoulders. His frigate’s mast has fallen into Davey Jones’ Locker and his shipmates are havin’ to form a human tower so they can get a lookout up top to survey the seas. But the deck’s wobblin’ and with every pirate stacked comes the chance of dippin’ into the deep. Take turns stacking pirates and the last player to add to the tower without making it collapse wins!

—description from the publisher

Packed inside one deck of cards is all the fun and nerve-racking energy of Screwball Scramble, the classic all-action challenge from TOMY Games. Start out with the 12 cards that make up the Standard Game and build your own obstacle course, with the Start card at one end and the Finish at the other. Lay out the track to form a 4 x 3 card grid as set for the Standard game. On your turn, shake the dice - you’ll need to match the combination shown on the card to move forward one space to the next card and the next. For instance, you might need to roll a 2 or higher, or roll two different numbers in three rolls. If you can’t, too bad. It’s your opponent’s turn. First to the finish wins. When you’re ready, use the full deck where Skill cards add or subtract one or two points to your roll – or surprise your opponent by changing theirs: 1-ON-1 will have two players battling on a freestyle course, or EXTREME is the game variation where each player designs their own course! Any which way, it’s a Screwball Scramble from start to finish!

—description from the publisher

In Ctrl Global, players lead powerful organizations seeking to dominate the planet’s political, economic, military, religious, media, and biological sectors. Use your resources and influence to expand your control, exploit regions, and strengthen your global power while staying under the radar of civil society.

Each turn, spend Energy, Raw Materials, and Food to boost your influence, develop regions, or activate your faction’s powers. Play Action cards to infiltrate new areas, move or place extractors, and trigger strategic effects that can disrupt your rivals. The Civil Society reacts when global corruption rises, striking back at the most exploitative players.

The game ends when a player reaches Level 5 in any category or uses all their influence cubes. Points are scored from regional development, world centers, and influence majorities. The most dominant organization at the end of the game wins.

A satirical strategy game of power, manipulation, and global domination.

—description from the publisher

You are popular influencers posting pictures of cute birds. And now, taking pictures of little birds with sweets, called Tori(bird)-Tori(sweet) photo are the craze on the streets! 
You drop by to a well-known bird café "Tori-Bar-Do", to not be late to the trend. 
Photo shooting many charming photos, you post Tori-Tori photos every day. Although be careful because trends always change! Check the ever changing trends and post the outstanding best photo against your rivals! This is a card game with simple rules, you want to get a high score by manipulating the trend ranking.

Each player will be dealt 7 photo cards (each card is a unique combination of a bird and a treat). During setup, 12 trend cards (6 birds and 6 treats) will be lined up randomly from top to bottom, birds and treats separately, with the top being marked by the top trend card. A round of the game takes 6 turns. Every turn, players will play one of their cards to manipulate both trend rankings – as the cards depict both a bird and a treat, they will need to raise the rank of one of them and decrease the rank of the other (you move the trend cards only by one position up or down). Decreasing the rank of a card that is already at the bottommost place will put it at the top and raising the rank of a card that is at the topmost place will put it at the bottom of its line. After 6 turns, each player should be left with one photo card in their hand – this is their scoring card. Each player checks the trend position of the bird and treat on their photo card and calculates their score (the highest trend bird/treat card is worth 6 points, the second highest 5 points, and so on; so, for example, if your remaining photo card depicted a budgie and a donut and their corresponding trend placement was the budgie as the top bird trend and donut as the third highest treat trend, then your score would be 6 + 4 = 10 points). The player with the most points earns a score token. The game ends after the round in which one of the players earns his second score token.

There is also a variant to play the game with special “joker” cards, in which each player is dealt 8 cards instead of 7 (one of them is the joker card), and they choose between two remaining cards to score at the end of the round.

Shuffle the deck and deal everyone three cards facedown in a row. These are player's Code Cards. You may look at your Code Cards but keep this three digit Code facedown. Deal three cards to each player's hand.

On your turn you may perform one action:

Draw, then discard a card.

Play a pair of numbers. Select one of the two colors and choose a player. If their Code contains a number of chosen color they must flip one face up.

Play a pair of colors. Select a number of that color and choose a player. If their Code contains a number of that color they must flip it face up.

Guess another player's three digit Code. If you correctly guess all digits, they flip and are out. If any are incorrect you must flip one of your Code Cards.

Always draw back up to three cards at the end of your turn.

The last player with a hidden Code wins.

Simple Setup: discard cards in separate piles based on color. Players may look through these piles anytime.

Long Variant: Deal each player 5 Code Cards instead of 3.

Pillow Bash from TOMY Games will get you thinking, ducking and diving. It’s stuffed full of brilliant games to test your wits and skills as you go head-to-head in a series of pillow fights with each of your opponents. Who’ll have a heavy landing and who’ll come out on top? On your turn, pick out a random feather from the pillow pocket. White feathers will see you sink your teeth into a category game. You just need to come up with words and answers that fit the category. Bat ideas back and forth with your opponent. If you hesitate, go blank or repeat, you lose! Your opponent gets to give you a bashing with the pillow and you’re out. Typical things you will list include Things You Wear, Ice Cream Flavours, Dinosaurs, Stinky Stuff and more. If you pick out a Blue feather on your turn, you’ll play any one of a dozen Challenge games. It might be a round of Blinkety Blink where you stare at your opponent and try not to blink. Giggle Off where you try not to giggle. A race to find five objects, a round of Rock Paper Scissors and lots more. Nail it and you stay in the game. Last player still standing at the end will be the winner!

—description from the publisher

Deface Race is the speedy scribbling challenge that’s brilliant at bringing out the secret graffiti artist in you. Players split into two teams. On your turn as the artist, pick a picture card for everyone to see and glance at the three secret options on the next character card. Turn over the timer and start scribbling over the picture card with the dry-wipe pen. Can you start with a picture of a llama and get your team mates to guess what’s written on the character card? It might say “Fairy”, “Alien” or “Snow White”. Choose which one you’ll doodle. Just a few lines and some shading could do it. As soon as they guess correctly, pick another picture card, sneak a peek at the next character card and go on sketching before the bell rings and time runs out! It’s now the other team’s turn to do the same. Players can agree how many rounds to play at the start, and how long the timer should run – you can set it for one or two minutes. The team with the most correct guesses at the end wins!

—description from the publisher

In this strategy game, you will guide the expansion of your family's farm beyond the known boundaries of the forest. As your settlement grows, more people will gather under your banner. You will cultivate the land, build new communities, and shape the future. However, you must proceed with caution. Your expansion may unsettle the spirits that still dwell in the wilderness. The yokai are vigilant, and their mischief can hinder your plans if you fail to recognize their presence. Increase your influence, adapt your strategies, and attract the right people to your settlement to gain victory points.

In Shugendo, each player has their own personal deck of cards. During the game, you will improve your deck by replacing your weaker starting cards with more powerful settler cards. In each round, players play three phases: reveal, action, and recruitment. In the first phase, they will reveal cards until they decide to stop or until they 'explode' by revealing 7 or more yokai icons. If this happens, they can only play either the action phase or the recruitment phase. In the action phase, players increase their meters based on the revealed cards and perform one of the main actions (explore, expand, or develop). Finally, in the recruitment phase, players receive various bonuses based on the revealed cards and recruit cards from the unlocked eras.

—description from the publisher

During the days leading up to Christmas, children receive and exchange gifts with one another as they race to collect a variety of presents. Each turn, you choose which die to roll—the red die for smaller steps or the green die for longer strides—then move along a cozy, festive, hand-drawn path.

Land on a clock and the calendar advances; land on a present and you receive a gift from under the tree. You can swap gifts at any time, even during other players’ turns. The goal is set collection: build a set containing each different symbol—Santa, Stocking, Mistletoe, and Candy Cane—for exponential scoring payoffs, all while avoiding coal, which subtracts points. Alternatively, collect all six coal to immediately win the game and be crowned “The Grouch That Stole Christmas.”

Simple to learn and delightfully interactive, Race to Christmas blends classic roll-and-move nostalgia with modern set collection and open trading.

Designer Note: Originally created when the designer was just ten years old, Race to Christmas has been lovingly brought to life decades later with modern artwork and refined rules— all while keeping the magic of the childhood Christmas spirit alive.

—description from the designer

The Moretti family just lost their Mob Boss, and the job is up for grabs! Now is your chance to score some reputation with the Moretti family and prove once and for all you are a true gangster. You should grab a few jobs to prove your worth and eliminate your competition while you have the chance. If you can get enough reputation with the family, who knows how far you will go.

Capodecina is a fun family game where players try to take each other down in attempts to gain reputation with the Moretti family.

This quick card game is perfect for parties or for family game nights.

—description from the designer

Get ready for spinning and grinning action with Phil the Fridge! This fun game is easy to understand, making it a great choice for family game night. Just push Phil’s head down to set him spinning. Each time he stops, quickly fill him up with your colored food pieces. But be quick and get them in before he goes for another spin! As you’re playing, Phil will randomly pop open his door. Any of the colored food pieces that fall out must be collected by the player they belong to. Close the door and push his head to start him spinning again! Phil has four sides, but only three have food shaped holes. If Phil stops facing you, you’ll need to wait until he spins again for you turn to fill him up! Phil features both an easy and expert level of play. A longer pause is great for younger players and a shorter pause that makes filling him up even more challenging for older kids.

—description from the publisher

Las Mascaradas is inspired by the ancestral winter festivals of Zamora, a province in northern Spain, where villagers wear handmade masks and perform ancient rituals to drive away demons and ensure a fertile year. The game captures this vibrant mix of folklore, mystery, and celebration, inviting players to recreate the spirit of these rural masquerades.

During the game, players acquire cards from two dynamic markets — one for resources and one for masquerades. By combining elements strategically, they can trigger chain effects and create powerful scoring combos. Each decision shapes the procession’s rhythm and intensity, as players compete to assemble the most impressive parade.

The goal is simple yet deeply tactical: build the best masquerade parade in the city, blending artistry, planning, and timing to outshine your rivals.

—description from the designer
language_dependency: 4: Extensive use of text - massive conversion needed to be playable

description:
Bloodrush is a quirky survival game from Fitz&Pops Tabletops featuring tile placement, resource management, and push-your-luck mechanisms where vampires hunt for humans in a Far West frontier town. Players strategically place human tiles during setup, then navigate the board using blood as both currency and life force while managing an autonomous mosquito threat that follows programmed movement patterns. The game combines set collection with color-matching bonuses, wraparound "Mario pipe" movement for tactical positioning, and a burst mechanism that punishes greedy blood hoarders, creating a light yet strategic experience where players must balance aggressive hunting with careful resource management to survive until dawn.

—description from the publisher

SETUP
Follow these steps to prepare your game:
Phase 1: Determine Turn Order
The player who bled last will be Player 1 (starts first)
The next player clockwise will be Player 2 (places the Mozzie on the board)
Remaining players continue clockwise for turn order
Phase 2: Dynamic Human Placement
Shuffle all 10 human tiles face-down into a stack
Flip the entire stack face-up so only the top tile is visible
Players take turns placing one human tile at a time in turn order
Placement Rule: Humans can be placed anywhere on the board EXCEPT tiles immediately adjacent to the graveyard
Humans can be placed on special tiles (Damage, Clocks, Steps)
Continue until all 10 humans are placed
Phase 3: Final Setup
Player 2 places the Mozzie anywhere on the board
Each player places their Vampire tile in the graveyard
Each player starts with 5 blood tiles
Set the time tracker to dusk (starting position)
Shuffle the card deck
VICTORY CONDITIONS (RANKED BY PRIORITY)
The game concludes when either only one vampire remains alive or all humans have been consumed from the board. Winners are determined in priority order:
Survival Victory - Last vampire standing wins immediately (regardless of human collection)
Collection Victory - Most human tiles collected when all are taken (count number of tiles)
Strategy Bonuses - If tied on tile count, calculate tile values and color-matching bonuses:
Base value: Sum of all collected human tile numbers (1-5)
2 humans same color = +1 bonus point
3+ humans same color = +3 bonus points
Resource Tiebreaker - Most blood tiles remaining
TURN STRUCTURE
Each vampire player's turn consists of:
1. PLAYER ACTION (Choose ONE):
Pay 1 blood tile to move 1 step - Move orthogonally (up, down, left, right - no diagonal movement)
Draw a card:
Black card: Roll the die and resolve the action
Red card: Keep it or play immediately (can be played at any time)
Collect 1 blood tile
2. MOZZIE CONTROL:
Choose direction: Straight, left, or right (no U-turn)
Roll the die: Move the Mozzie by that number of Steps
3. TIME EFFECTS (If Applicable):
Midnight Bonus: Receive blood if on moon symbol
Dawn Penalty: Pay blood if on sun symbol
SPECIAL CARDS
Black Cards (Action Cards - BLOODRUSH logo on back):
Coyote Snack (15 cards): Roll the die, take that amount of blood
Donkey Ride (10 cards): Roll the die, move that amount of Steps
Time Energy (5 cards): Roll the die, move the time forward by that amount
Red Cards (Special Cards - Can be played at any time):
Bug Commander: Prevents turning the Mozzie left or right (Mozzie can only go straight)
Giant Umbrella: Prevents damage during dawn penalty when played
Mask of Immunity: Prevents blood damage in any circumstance (Mozzie stepping on Damage tile or player stepping on Damage tile)
Carriage Ride: Move one human by one tile in any direction
BLOOD MANAGEMENT
Start with 5 blood tiles
Maximum capacity: 10 blood tiles
Cannot collect blood beyond maximum
Burst Mechanic: Exceeding 10 blood tiles reduces player to 1 tile and forces graveyard return
MOVEMENT AND NAVIGATION
Players act as walls - cannot occupy same tile
Movement is orthogonal only (up, down, left, right) - no diagonal movement
Wraparound Movement ("Mario Pipe"): Players can move seamlessly from any edge square to the opposite edge
MOVEMENT STOPPING RULE
When any creature (Vampire or Mozzie) encounters a target during movement, their movement stops immediately regardless of remaining Steps:
Vampire stepping on Human: Movement stops, collect human
Mozzie stepping on Human: Movement stops, human eliminated
Mozzie stepping on Vampire: Movement stops, vampire drained
Example: If a Mozzie is 1 tile away from a human and rolls 2 Steps, the Mozzie stops after moving 1 Step onto the human and does not continue for the remaining Step.
HUMANS AND FEEDING
When a vampire steps on a human tile:
Collect the tile
Gain 5 blood tiles (cannot exceed maximum of 10)
Hidden Hazard: If human was placed on a special tile, the underlying effect triggers BEFORE collecting the human's blood
MOZZIE RULES
Starting position chosen by Player 2 during setup
Controlled by players during their turns
Moves through all areas except graveyard (flies over)
Mozzie encounters:
Mozzie → Vampire: Vampire loses all blood except 1, Mozzie returns to starting position
Mozzie → Human: Human eliminated, Mozzie stays in current position
TIME SYSTEM
Dual Time Advancement:
Card-Based Movement: Time Energy cards advance time by die roll result
Round-Based Movement: Player 1 advances time by 1 hour at the start of each of their turns
Time Effects:
Midnight Bonus (moon symbols): Gain blood at end of turn
Dawn Penalty (sun symbols): Lose blood at end of turn
Protection: No dawn penalty when in graveyard, buildings, or mine
Mine Special Rule: Players in the mine are also immune to blood damage when Mozzie steps on Damage tiles
SPECIAL TILES
When Vampire Steps on Special Tile:
Damage tiles: Pay blood (triggers before human collection if human is present)
Clocks tiles: Time moves
Steps tiles: Free movement
When Mozzie Steps on Special Tile:
All vampires experience effect (except those protected):
Damage tiles: Everyone pays blood (except those in graveyard or mine)
Clocks tiles: Time moves
Steps tiles: Free movement for Mozzie
ELIMINATION
A vampire is eliminated when:
They run out of blood tiles completely
Burst: Exceeding 10 blood maximum (return to graveyard with 1 blood)

4+1 PLAYER VARIANT: MOZZIE PLAYER
This variant allows a 5th player to control the Mozzie exclusively for groups wanting to include everyone.
SETUP CHANGES:
Same as standard game, but one player is designated as the Mozzie Player
Mozzie Player does not receive a Vampire tile or blood tiles
TURN STRUCTURE WITH MOZZIE PLAYER:
Vampire Players: Only perform their action (no Mozzie control)
Mozzie Player: On their turn, control Mozzie direction and roll for movement
MOZZIE PLAYER ROLE:
The Mozzie Player plays for fun and group engagement
No specific victory condition - they participate to enable 5-player games
Their goal is to create chaos and challenge for the vampire players

Crownfell: Last Stand is an 18-card solo expansion for Crownfell.

With a mere four anti-heroes, outmaneuver a relentless tide of 13 unique enemies.

Enemy actions are determined by a simple card flip, providing smooth gameplay without complex decision trees.

Each enemy performs the topmost action that it’s possible to take from their current position. Any time there’s a decision to make about which space or card to target, use the priority arrow on the back of the flipped card.

Your goal is to capture all 13 enemy cards with just four cards of your own.

—description from the designer

Dive into Coral Clash, a chess-like strategy game where you command an aquatic army of dolphins, turtles, and more, to outmaneuver your opponent in a war to create and own the coral reef. Victory is achieved through clever strategy: either checkmate your opponent's whale or dominate the board with coral control.

The primary objective in Coral Clash is to either checkmate your opponent's Whale or control the most area with your Coral when specific conditions are met, such as placing all your Coral or having only your Whale remaining. Every piece has unique movement patterns as well as a role. Gatherers are able to place coral on the board, the way to win the area of control part of the game. Hunters are able to remove coral, but their movement is stopped by coral.

Perfect for fans of chess, abstract strategy games, and ocean adventures!

—description from the publisher

The UNO game players love features graphics inspired by characters from Disney's Lilo and Stitch. Game play matches the classic card game where players match colors and numbers to get rid of all their cards. The special 'Ohana' rule lets players with more than 4 cards in their hand discard down to 4, and player with less than 4 cards in their hand draw up to 4. When down to one card, don't say, 'Aloha' -- shout 'UNO!'

—description from the publisher

Play Ahoy There!, the card game that turns every player into a pirate! Steal cards from your neighbours, keep the best ones for your hoard of booty, then pass your hand overboard to the next player to see who’ll sink or swim. Dig for gems and a Holy Grail, look out for curses or losing all your loot. Stash the most treasure to win! 2-5 players, 6 years and up.

Ahoy There! can take you high into the crow’s nest or see you sink deep down into Davy Jones’ locker! Pirates play round by round in a nerve-jangling battle of wits and tricks. Each buccaneer sets sail with a hand of Loot cards. When the captain shouts “Ahoy There!” it’s all hands on deck. Pick a card from the pirate to your left and add it to your hand. The next sailor to their left does the same and so on until everyone has chosen a card at random. Now use strategy and skill to decide which card to stash in your Treasure Chest – the pile of booty in front of you – and which cards to keep. Some are worth more than others, some spell trouble, some will be worth keeping in circulation. Next, everyone passes their hand to the player on their right! Time for the next round, this time with a brand new set of cards and choices! Keep playing until each pirate is down to one card. Look out for “Give ‘Em All Yer Gold”, a card that forces you to sacrifice your spoils. “Curses” mean trouble too, with points deducted at the end. “Skull & Crossbones” means you need to return a card to the deck. Then there are “Necklace” and “Gem” cards – collect a set and you’ll boost your booty. Land the “Holy Grail” card and you’ll discover it’s worth much more than a few doubloons… Tot up your points at the end and see which pirate has heaped the biggest haul to win!

—description from the publisher

boardgame: Vae Victis: A Game of Roman Might and Gallic Fury
description:
This rule set emphasizes the distinct asymmetric nature of the two armies, focusing on Roman discipline versus Gaulish individual prowess and numerical advantage.

It is designed to be a quick skirmish game with simple mechanics but also one in which tactical acumen is required. The figure scale is agnostic although I find the larger 54mm and 75mm scale figures work well and provide a larger visual spectacle.

As indicated above the game features an asymmetric conflict between the disciplined Roman forces and the fierce Gauls. Romans move on in column formation on a short table edge, and any casualties they take are permanent. The Gauls, however, stream onto the board from any long table edge and their casualties are not permanent; they can re-enter the game on the next turn, representing their vast numbers. Movement speeds reflect this difference: Gallic figures, including cavalry and chariots, move a full stick, while Roman infantry moves only a half stick.

The core of the game-play is the unpredictable Chaos of War activation system. 
The game utilises a deck containing one specific card for every unit on the board, plus a single Joker. Players shuffle this deck and draw cards sequentially. When a Unit Card is drawn, that unit immediately takes its single action (Move, Shoot, or Charge). The entire Activation Phase, and thus the opportunity for any remaining units to move, immediately ends if the Joker is drawn, forcing players to react constantly to the possibility of a sudden turn halt. There is no simultaneous movement phase, making the card draw the sole determinant of action.

Once the Activation Phase ends, either by the deck running out or the Joker being drawn, the turn has ended. There is no melee phase, combat occurs if at the end of a models activation they are in base to base combat with an opponent.. 
Combat uses a special skull/shield dice (Milton Bradley Battle Master game dice). The base number of dice rolled contrasts sharply: Gauls roll 3 dice, while Romans roll only 1 die. However, Romans gain crucial bonuses for formation, notably +1 die if their Signifier (standard bearer is present, and +1 die for every friend in base-to-base contact, rewarding tight, disciplined lines. Kills also favour the disciplined; a Gaul is removed by two uncancelled skulls, but a Roman requires three. Javelins are resolved only in melee, and the only long-range attack is the bow, which fires two dice at a range of two movement sticks.

This system creates a game where the Romans must leverage every bonus to survive the inevitable charge and hold the line, while the Gauls rely on their speed, high individual attack power, and the terrifying knowledge that their fallen warriors will soon return to the fray.

boardgame: Fireball Forward: World War II Raids
description:
Fireball Forward: World War II Raids

The scenarios are drawn primarily from the Osprey Publishing  Raid Series of monographs. The Scenarios include:


     Ham and Jam! - British Paras capture and hold Pegasus Bridge on D-Day in France
     Makin Atoll Raid - Carlson's Raiders hit Japanese-held Makin Island in the Gilbert Islands
     The Bruneval Raid - British Paras steal Hitler's radar from the coast of France
     The Vaagso Raid - British Commandos strike German occupation troops in Norway
     Kill Rommel! - British Commandos go after General Rommel in Libya
     Qui Va La?! - Darby's Rangers night attack the Italian Bersaglieri in Tunisia
     Team Barlow's Raid - U.S. tanks surprise a German airfield in Tunisia
     Raid on Tuguegaro Airfield - The Philippine Scouts strike deep at a Japances airfield
     Drop Zone Manado -  Japanese paratroopers seize a Dutch airfield in the Celebes
     Rescuing Mussolini - The SS and Fallschirmjeagers in gliders free the jailed Fascist Dictator


Player Aids include:
Scenario Notes
Scenario-specific Unit Activation Markers
Objective occupation markers
Templates for special scenario play
Reference charts
Scenario-specific large-sized maps for tracking hidden deployment / movement

A party game dedicated to pointless debates.

Very active on social media, we regularly organize games. For over a year and a half, we've had a must-attend event: the Tuesday Pointless Debate.

The goal? To vote and provide a convincing (or absurd) argument in a poll we choose, on a topic of little importance.

We've finally decided to turn this true icon of online entertainment into a board game.

Rules:

     Each round is played with three players: two debaters and a referee.
     The objective is to score points by winning "Pointless Debate" cards or "Special" cards.
     The referee draws a "Pointless Debate" card, announces the debate to the two debaters, and designates who will defend which point of view.
     The two debaters take turns arguing until they run out of arguments or the referee decides it's over.
     The referee declares the winner. Each debater can also earn points through "null arguments," which are more or less logical arguments submitted by our community.
     The "special" cards contain 15 mini-games that will challenge your imagination and creativity.


Un jeu de société d’ambiance made in France dédié aux débats futiles.

Très actifs sur nos réseaux sociaux, nous organisons régulièrement des jeux. Depuis plus d’un an et demi, nous avons un rendez-vous incontournable : le débat nul du mardi.

Le but ? Voter et donner un argument convaincant (ou absurde) lors d’un sondage que nous choisissons, sur un sujet sans grande importance.

De ce véritable monument du divertissement virtuel, nous avons enfin décidé d’en faire un jeu de société.

Règles :

     Chaque tour se joue à trois : deux débateurs et un arbitre.
     L’objectif est de gagner des points en remportant les cartes « débats nuls » ou les cartes « spéciales ».
     L’arbitre pioche une carte « débat nul », annonce le débat aux 2 débateurs et désigne qui défendra quel point de vue.
     Les deux débateurs argumentent chacun leur tour jusqu’à ce qu’ils n’aient plus d’arguments ou que l’arbitre décide que c’est terminé.
     L’arbitre désigne le vainqueur. Chaque débatteur peut également remporter des points via les « arguments nuls », des raisonnements plus ou moins logiques provenant de notre communauté.
     Les cartes « spéciales », elles, contiennent 15 mini-jeux qui feront appel à votre imagination et votre créativité.

Players take turns being the Girl in Need by reading a Situation card, while the remaining players, known as the Girl Squad, choose a Solution card and explain why they have the best answer.  The player who can sell in their solution earns a a point.  Earn the most points and win the game!

Includes 280 cards and rules.

Get ready to tap your toes to the beat with 400 questions on songs, bands, artists, and all things musical!  Divided into categories on pop, rock, movie soundtracks, and the hits of the 80s, go head-to-head with friends and family to see if you're a musical maestro or a one-hit wonder!

boardgame: Appalachian Trail Game: Thru-Hiker Expansion Cards
description: Adds 85 Challenge, 10 Identification, and 15 Chance cards to the base game.

boardgame: Appalachian Trail Game: A.T. Communities Expansion
description: Adds 85 Challenge, 10 Identification, and 15 Chance cards to the base game.

boardgame: Circino: le Chasseur de Trésors – Destination Isère
description:
How well do you know the towns of Isère? Our little adventurer Circino doesn't think so...

This is the goal of this board game, in addition to allowing children, their parents, and grandparents to spend pleasant moments together in a friendly and cheerful atmosphere.

To ensure everyone can discover these towns while having fun, Créacom Games opted for an intergenerational theme: a treasure hunt.

36 towns in the department were chosen to be featured in the game with their "Objective" card and corresponding "Treasure."

Each town chose its own two photos and wrote its own introductory text.

The goal of the game is to find the two Treasures (gold coins) corresponding to the two towns chosen at the beginning of the game, then to bring them back to Circino first in the center of the board.

To do this, players will have to search the various chests armed only with dice and their sense of adventure (memory)... using secret passages and tunnels, and using boosters.

In their quest, players will not be alone and can count on the help of members of the Circino family through Bonus cards.

—description from the publisher (translated)

Connaissez-vous bien les communes de l'Isère ? Notre petit aventurier Circino ne le pense pas ...

Tel est donc l'objectif recherché par ce jeu de plateau, en plus de permettre aux plus petits, leurs parents et leurs grands-parents d'occuper d'agréables moments de partage dans la convivialité et la bonne humeur.

Pour que chacun puisse découvrir ces communes tout en s'amusant, Créacom Games a opté pour un thème intergénérationnel : une chasse aux trésors.

36 communes du département ont donc été choisies pour être mises en avant dans le jeu avec sa carte "Objectif" et son "Trésor" correspondant.

Chaque commune a choisi elle-même ses 2 photos et a rédigé son texte de présentation.
