Game play revolves around the interaction of units which represent companies or platoons of infantry, infantry support elements, squadrons of armoured fighting vehicles, aircraft and artillery or anti-tank guns in either batteries or operating as individual elements. Whilst higher command and organisation are present, and units can influencing each other, the morale and fighting effectiveness is based on the individual unit itself.

You will need a set of polyhedral dice (d4, d6, d8, d10 and d12), an artillery directional dice (scatter dice), a tape measure and a sense of fair play. It does not contain army lists or point costs but rather aims to enable scenarios to reflect researched and historical or historically plausible table-top encounters. You are encouraged to develop your own unit cards and examples are scattered throughout these rules as a guide.

—description from the rulebook
family: Players: Wargames with Rules Supporting Only Two Players

description:
Catacombs is a cooperative adventure game featuring dice-rolling mechanics and procedurally generated dungeons, where you play as a hero venturing into the darkness of a catacombs to slay and defeat evil. 
All characters are cats.
Traverse dungeons, race against the clock, summon evil, and defeat it solo or in a team of 2, 3, or 4. To win, you must find and light all the summoning candles in the dungeon. After that, you'll face the final dungeon boss. Defeating him secures victory.

—description from the publisher

Living Hell is a competitive two-player LCG (Living Card Game) where each player chooses one of two factions (Horde and Reign) and uses a 40-card deck featuring Creatures, Spells, Weapons, Traps, and Boost.

The gameplay is strategic and fast-paced, focusing on battlefield control and card synergies. Matches are intense duels in which players try to bring the opponent’s 20 life points down to zero through creature attacks or spell damage.

As an LCG, Living Hell grows through planned expansions, offering a balanced and customizable experience without random booster packs.

The game also introduces mechanics that set it apart from others in the genre. There is no mana, sacrifices, or resource buildup—each player can play one creature per turn with no extra requirements, keeping the action moving. It also features a unique card, “Baal,” which triggers once enough creatures have been sent to the graveyard. This forces a final decisive hand where players must rely solely on what they draw, adding tension and a dynamic endgame.

In Pactopolis, players represent 4 leading industries that have been tasked by the Council of the lands (Aka - the government) with developing a vast natural land area consisting of various types of terrain (mountains, forests, lakes, grasslands, meadows and the sea). In a game with asymmetric information - each industry has their own needs and bonuses - the players will have to smartly negotiate to balance their needs, others needs, environmental needs and government needs. In this game the level of cooperation and/or collaboration is determined by the players themselves. Resources are dependent on each sectors growth. Each sector is also dependent on the growth of the other sectors. Land may seem plentiful or not - depending on your situation (and sometimes a little luck). Every game plays different and if you play with different people you will have to learn to adapt your stratgies based on how your opponents play! Is it a cooperative or a com,petitive game. It is both. There is only one winner but if you dont learn to cooperate - everybody loses. This is also a game where even the losers don't always feel bad about losing as they do win something! The negotiations get more complex as the game progresses. Time limits are set for the negotiation rounds (which are free-for-all type of negotiations). Plan ahead what you need as you can ONLY negotiate in the negotiation rounds! Sustainable technologies (based on real-world information) can be unlocked with clever negotiations to earn you rare negotiation points (this is the only non-tradeable item in the entire game). 
This is an edge-of-the-seat negotiations game where as the game progresses the negotiations get more complex! It is a long(ish) yet highlighy addictive game (feedback from multiple gamers)- easy to get started but tough to easily end as the game continues over 5 rounds to find out the winner! You can simply win or win smart! Welcome to Pactopolis - a world where smart negotiation meets sustainable innovation!

—description from the publisher

What lies behind the curtain?
 Life in the royal court is full of secrets, danger and drama. To survive here the members of the court must communicate in secret by giving and receiving coded hints. To keep their skills sharp, they play Entracte.

The common goal of the players is to correctly guess as many of the other player’s codes as they can. To do so they take turns playing character cards into the row and place signal tokens below suitable cards. They also analyze the actions of the other players in an attempt to guess their codes.

— Play cards with portraits of members of the royal court. Each of them contains a unique set of characteristics.
— Place tokens below pairs of cards the characteristics of which match your secret code.
— Compete with each other or work as a team. Uncover the secret codes of all players to win!

The game has two modes: cooperative and competitive.

—description from the publisher
This ten-card expansion adds a new pair of animals and eight more action cards that allows for a fifth and sixth player.

In this sentence-construction game, players are dealt a hand of double-sided cards, each with three words per side. Over three rounds players take turns discarding and drawing cards, and when they can construct a sentence, play those cards on the table, lining up the lines on the cards. Each word has a point value, and the first person to reach one hundred points wins.
This ten-card expansion adds two more planets and their eight items, allowing for a fifth and sixth player.

In this sentence-finishing game, players take turns drawing a card from the deck. Each card contains a sentence missing one word, with a list of potential words to complete the sentence, each word having a point value, increasing with the length of the word. The other players try to guess what one these words are, and the person who guesses the largest word earns points. When a card has been drawn for the third time, the game ends and the person with the most points wins.
This ten-card expansion adds more penguins and icebergs, including a special whale card, the allows for a fifth and sixth player.

Minikin City is a strategic game where every card you play has a double meaning. Colors and values give you rewards each round, while icons and effects give you points at the end, but only if you fill the card with Minikins!

Reward cards at the center determine the best color for each round. The cards played in that round determine the players' ranking.

Choose to play a card with a high value in the best color to get the most powerful reward, or play a card with an effect you like... or both!

Many effects on the cards and rewards at the center give you Minikins that you can place on your cards. At the end of the game, only the cards that you have filled with the required number of Minikins will activate their scoring effect.
Create your cute city in this clever card game!

—description from the publisher
This ten-card expansion adds action cards for more strategic depth and enough money cards for a fifth and sixth player.
This adds ten action cards, allowing one to cancel a round, remove sidekicks or all of an opponent's team, play extra cards, or draw a new hand.
This ten-card expansion adds three new friends with matching question cards.
This adds ten Scary Scarecrows to the deck to make the grid even larger.

Two groups (Apostles and Devils) try to reach different goals. The apostle group try to reach certain fields on the map while the devils try to prevent them from reaching it. 
The devil group consist of 3 devils and the apostle group consist of 12 apostles, 5 cherubim, 26 yellow angles and 7 blue angles. Each figure has a different movement. By blocking or removing figures one can reach his goal.
The apostle group win, when all 12 apostle and all 5 cherubim reach the golden points in the heaven area.

A typical German board game for its time.

boardgame: Once Upon a Line: Les Larmes de Fond
description:
This extension contains two fundamental mechanical differences from the original saga. The first concerns the Heroes.

From the start of the adventure, you will be able to identify and embody eight individuals who are more talented than the others. A strong but inexplicable connection seems to bind them together. Pilot, special forces soldier, psychiatrist... each excels in their field and will give you an undeniable advantage on the battlefield.

However, this immediate connection with all these Heroes makes your mental bond less powerful. Only time spent alongside each of them will improve their performance. Your Heroes start with very few standard tiles.

Fortunately, each of their talents comes with an additional standard tile that is very useful for progressing on the Destiny Grids. Each Power Word will therefore be all the more valuable to you in succeeding in your quest.

boardgame: Once Upon a Line: La Transe des Poissons d'Argent
description:
This saga contains 4 scenarios.

With the exception of the last one, play each scenario in any order you like! Transe Macabre is the final chapter of this story, delivering an epic conclusion worthy of a short saga rich in discoveries.

In each scenario, you always play as Frigan and a character linked to him and the story.

boardgame: Once Upon a Line: Splendeur & Déchéance
description:
From the stench of the sewers to the opulent boxes of the Amber Opera, experience the heavenly rise of some and the descent into the abyss of others.
From darkness to light, discover the hidden side of the World of Kell.

Rule Changes:
In this expansion, become Soufflerimes on probation. No major tragedies on the horizon, but a more or less bright future for the saga's atypical characters. Complete your Quest to unlock new playable characters in the main saga!

—description from the publisher (translated)

The deck is laid out in a 7x3 grid face down to create the kingdom. Players take turns flipping over one card: dragon or people cards remain in place, while coin cards are collected to a player's hand, and can later be used to hire the services of knights, wizards, or alchemists, which provide special bonuses. The goal is to catch the most dragons by using your team of hirelings, and the person with the highest value catch wins.

A Days deck is shuffled and each player receives twelve face-up cards in a 2x6 grid, representing the "days of Christmas" from the traditional song. A Moves deck is then placed in the center of the table, and players take turns flipping over one card, which determines how many cards a player may swap: either with a card from an opponent's grid, or within one's own grid, to try to put the cards in numerical order from 1-12. The first person to do this wins.

Kimbap Klash is a fast, flavorful K-food card battler where players race to craft the ultimate kimbap roll. Draft tasty ingredients, dodge (or weaponize!) yucky ones, and stir up chaos with action cards that let you steal, swap, or sabotage.

At the end of the game, everyone reveals their hidden character card, triggering a unique power that can completely flip the table. Stack smart combos, chase bold flavors, and build the most creative roll you can — because only the highest-scoring kimbap earns bragging rights!

—description from the publisher

Roll a die and decorate your Christmas Tree.

The aim is to be the first player to complete your Christmas Tree by rolling a die and placing counters in the appropriate space.

Once play begins, each player rolls their individual die. Each number has a corresponding colour, as indicated by the pips on the die. Once you've completely covered all the empty spaces, you win.

Instructions include two alternate ways to play: Time Challenge, where you try and fill in as many spaces as possible in a pre-determined time limit and Roll Battle, where players roll all 6 dice at once and see who can complete their tree in the least amount of rolls.

Austerlitz – The Battle of the Three Emperors
Experience one of history’s greatest military masterpieces in this fully featured Marshals Unleashed scenario pack. Step onto the frozen fields of Moravia and command the armies of France, Russia, and Austria in a breathtaking recreation of the Battle of Austerlitz.
The Austerlitz battlefield map is included in two high-quality, printable formats:

     EU Size: 149 cm × 89 cm
     US Size: 33 × 59.5 inches


We highly recommend having the map professionally printed at your local print shop for the best gameplay experience.

To play the full scenario, the armies of France, Russia, and Austria are required.
This release also introduces new commanders for your army packs:

     France
         Marshal Murat
         Marshal Lannes
         Marshal Davout
         Marshal Soult
    
     Russia
         Prince Bagration
         General Langeron
         General Dokhturov
    


All corresponding unit strips are included in the download, ready to print and cut

Also included:

     The complete Austerlitz Order of Battle
     A full Commander Chart with command ranges
     The official gameplay timeline
     Additional gaming icons and tokens for expanded play

Hand made from natural stone, Gravitas is a competitive and artistic stacking game.  Players take turns rolling a die to identify stone placement on a growing stack.  Suspense and beauty builds with each players addition to the tower.  Skill development and strategy directly correspond to a player’s ability to make difficult and amazing balances.

In the set up phase, players strategically or aesthetically select their initial pieces from a diverse array of 36 varied stones.  Every game piece is hand cut and unique, and each game set offers over 1.9 million different options for any one player’s starting supply of pieces.

In the play phase, players roll a die to identify which piece, or combination of pieces, they must play.  If a player is unable to play their die roll, a strategic opportunity presents in carefully selecting which tower piece you remove and add to your supply - hint: it doesn’t need to be the piece that’s on top!  Whether friction, balance, or unique details of individual stone pieces hold the tower together, it only takes one misstep to bring it all down.  Any fallen pieces are added to the supply of the player who tipped them.

Some players choose to play collaboratively in pursuit of art and precarious balance, and some play competitively, setting traps and using slopes, pivots, or cantilevers to eliminate areas for opponent placement.  A player wins when they use their last remaining piece… unless gravity intervenes.

—description from the publisher

A chaotic minefield that's constantly changing as players set, upgrade, and destroy traps. It's your job to be the most powerful team in this fast-paced match where every trip around the board is more dangerous than the last! Traps are placed to stay on the board and affect players. They can be upgraded to boost their effects, or they can change into new traps entirely. Powers grant special abilities that can change the course of the battle. Games can be with nearby friends or globally against anyone else. Play across multiple platforms and devices and access your progress anywhere, any time. PowerTrip is a free-to-play game. All you need is a board. Get one for free.

—description from the designer

This is a small expansion to the base game that allows for solo play. It did not make it into the base game box following the 2022 Kickstarter crowdfunding campaign and was subsequently sent to backers about a month after initial fulfillment. It is also available on the publisher's webstore as an individual purchase.

Contains a single-sided rules page in color, a white standard six-sided die with black pips, and a solo deck containing 30 cards (mini-euro sized).

In 1880: Romania, based on 1880: China, the players become railway barons in Romania, experiencing the country's railway history in a game that was designed to represent related historical events as correctly as possible within its own game mechanisms. Being a classic 18xx game, the players compete to become the richest. In order to gain money, they buy and sell shares of Romania's historical railway companies, build and expand their railway network, and let trains travel on the networks. Shares of successful companies are worth more, and owning them will increase the chance to win the game. In the end, the player with the highest wealth combined from cash and shares wins.

1880: Romania uses the mechanisms of its sister game 1880: China which uses the 1829 rules from Francis Tresham as a base, and features a few innovations:


    Turn order for companies doesn't change with the evolving stock prices; it is set along with the par price when a player opens a company.
    The game includes a player-manipulated ratio of stock rounds and operation rounds. For example, a new stock round occurs when the last of a type of train is bought.
    Foreign investors develop track independently and once they are connected to a railway, they add their accumulated wealth to that railway
    The historical border between Austria-Hungary and Romania cannot be crossed in the yellow phase


1880: Romania offers the full experience as its Chinese sister game, but with a smaller map and significantly less playtime. A special map used for 2 and 3 player games can be found on the back of the regular map.

—description from the designer
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

description:
Its a railway game in the tradition from other 18xx games, but without laying tiles on a map.
Players invest in railway companies. They generate income with his trains. The player with the most shares in a railway company is its director. He decides whether this income will be distributed to shareholders as a dividend or whether it will be added to the company's treasury for future investments.
In the end, the player with the highest wealth combined from cash and shares wins.

—description from the designer

boardgame: Iron Abyss: Cold War Submarine Combat 1947 to 1991
description:
Iron Abyss: The Cold War Submarine Wargame: Dive into the silent, high-stakes world of anti-submarine warfare with Iron Abyss, the gripping two-player wargame set during an alternate-history Cold War (1947-1991). Designed for ease of play yet offering tactical depth, this game is an exercise in tension, secrecy, and critical decision-making.

Gameplay and Mechanics: The core of the experience is a hidden-information duel. Players start the game unaware of their opponent's forces, relying on confidential data cards and operation panels to track hidden information. The game board itself is abstracted, focusing on the thrilling cat-and-mouse hunt rather than precise measurements. Vessel models are placed on the table only when they are detected. Exact positions and distances are not important; weapons are always considered in range and movement is handled through abstract mechanics driven by the players.

Dynamic Decision-Making: The turn structure is designed to keep you constantly engaged. You can perform actions on both your turn and your enemy's turn using pre-assigned action dice, demanding continuous, impactful choices. Reactive actions, like Acoustic Defence or counter-firing, allow you to disrupt your opponent's plans at crucial moments, making every move a calculated risk.

Stealth and Detection: Secrecy is paramount. Vessels begin the game undetected, represented only by generic markers after initial contact. Building a "targeting solution" requires successful sonar searches to identify the enemy and gain the upper hand. This focus on incomplete information means players must use their limited intelligence to make vital tactical decisions, simulating the intense environment of real submarine combat.

Large Database of Ships and Subs: The game features an extensive database of accurately researched surface ships and submarines from the era, including US, Soviet, British, and many other nations' fleets. Each vessel has unique stats for sonar, noise, and anti-ship and anti-sub weapons, allowing for detailed and historically plausible scenario creation. The game includes 21 scenarios, including a hunt for the Belgrano and Soviets chasing a defecting SSBN, “Give me a ping Vasily”.

Accessible Rules: While deep in strategy, the ruleset is designed to be easily understandable. An "open information" mode is recommended for the first few learning games, ensuring a smooth entry for new players before diving into the full experience. Command a force of subs, ships, or a mixture, and use your wit and strategy to win the underwater war. Can you become the hunter, or will you be the hunted?

—description from the publisher
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups
family: Players: Wargames with Rules Supporting Only Two Players

description:
You are the founder of an underground settlement, striving to fulfill your ambitions and dreams of a glorious and wealthy kingdom. Use dice to construct chambers and tunnels as you work toward your goals, showcasing the glory and prosperity of your creation.

Underdice Kingdom is a solo dice game where you control the growth of an underground kingdom, built layer by layer with dice. Your goal is to complete the required kingdom shape and attributes before running out of moves. The game is played in around 15 minutes, and was designed for 2025 Solitaire Print and Play Contest. Grab your favorite dice in few different types/colors (variable quantity) and start playing, just dice, no more components required!

—description from the designer

The year is 2393, humanity reached the stars and grown a lot to became a galactic republic. Each planet in the republic needs to be connected with others to exchange goods and fulfill their needs. Thanks to the tech advancement there is an easy way to achieve that. Ships can travel to a distant places by using gravity folding provided by space stations controlled by the Gravio guild. Logistics was never easy though and planets can't wait forever. Would you pick the challange and become the greatest logistics manager of the republic?

Gravio is a solo dice manipulation puzzle game. Player take role of logistics manager of Gravio guild placing gravity folders to allow connections between distant planets of the galaxy. Fulfill planet requirements and make them prosperous and you will win. Leave planets unhappy causing unrest and you will loose. Will you handle this task or fail while trying?

Designed for the 2024 9-Card Nanogame Print and Play Design Contest.

Anastyr Chronicles is a cooperative campaign game set in a dark and savage fantasy world. You play as the Companions of the Spear, heroes bound by fate who journey across the lands of Alboraan in search of the fragments of the World Spear, an ancient artifact powerful enough to defeat Anastyr, the Dragon Queen, and her dragon Volkar.

Each Adventure unfolds on a modular board that evolves through a scrolling system inspired by beat 'em up video games, simulating continuous movement across shifting landscapes. Along their journey, heroes can even mount wild creatures, harnessing their abilities to move faster or strike with greater impact.

The game blends exploration and tactical combat against enemies controlled by the game. Heroes must coordinate their actions, manage their Combat cards to trigger combos or unleash their Fury, a devastating ability unique to each character. From one Adventure to the next, players unlock new Combat cards and grow in strength as they confront increasingly formidable foes, including Bosses.

The story is driven by Quest cards, which structure the objectives, develop the narrative, and introduce tension, twists, and evolving situations.

—description from the designer

Welcome to the magical world of Mushroom Shepherds! These mysterious creatures, guardians of the forest, have an ancient mission: to care for and guide the mushrooms in order to preserve the life of the undergrowth.

In Under Forest, you are a Mushroom Shepherd with a secret mission assigned to you by an Objective Card. Your goal is to build the forest on the table while completing as many of the three missions on your secret card as possible. On your turn, you take one of the two tiles available in the “Seedbed.” Then, you place that tile next to another already in play, deciding whether it will be placed face up as a Mushroom or face down as a Xilaria. Special tiles with the “⟳” symbol force you to flip an adjacent tile. At the end of your turn, the Seedbed is replenished. The game ends when the bag is empty. The player with the most points from completed missions wins!

—description from the publisher (translated)


Bienvenidos al magico mundo de los pastores de setas! Estas misteriosas criaturas, guardianas del bosque, tienen una mision ancestral: cuidar y guiar las setas para preservar la vida del sotobosque.

En Under Forest sos un pastor de setas con una misión secreta que se te asigna con una Carta de Objetivo. El objetivo es armar el bosque en la mesa cumpliendo la mayor cantidad posible de las 3 misiones de tu carta secreta. En tu turno, agarrás una de las dos losetas disponibles en "El Semillero". Luego, colocás esa loseta al lado de otra ya en juego, decidiendo si queda boca arriba como una Seta o boca abajo como una Xilaria. Las losetas especiales con el símbolo "⟳" te obligan a voltear una loseta adyacente. Al final de tu turno, se repone El Semillero. La partida termina cuando se agota la bolsa. ¡El jugador con más puntos por misiones cumplidas gana!.

—description from the publisher
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

description:
Sharks Have No Necks is a push-your-luck card game for 1-10 players.  Players turn over cards on their turn, and gain any points or stops.  Play continues until all players have been stopped or choose to stop.  The player with the most points at the end of three rounds is the winner.

—description from designer

NeuroSpicy - What's Your Flavor?
NeuroSpicy is a set building card game for 3-7 players where you determine your spice level.
Players start with one card. On their turn they can Save, Swap, or Discard/Draw. The goal is to collect a complete set of seven ingredients. The first player to collect a set ends the game. Players total the point values in their saved pile and score. But be careful, if you are over 15 points, you start losing them.

—description from the designer

Here's the plan: stack your bumbling orcs up a castle wall and reach the top. 
Easy peasy. 
Except your opponents had the exact same idea, and they've got boulders, tricks, and a competitive streak a mile wide.

Welcome to Orcs Up!, where controlled chaos is the name of the game.

Every round throws you into a tornado of frantic card plays, stacking, and climbing your way to victory while your tower constantly threatens to crumble.

Victory loves the bold.

—description from the publisher

boardgame: Cosmos Breakers: Epic 4X Solo Board Game
description:
Cosmos Breakers is a 4X space game set in a dark, retro sci-fi universe. Players explore a hostile galaxy filled with unknown systems, dangerous factions and ancient mysteries, shaping the rise and survival of their emerging Clan through each decision.

The core of the game focuses on fleet management and evolution, featuring a construction, upgrade and combat system that feels closer to a video game 4X experience than a traditional board game. Players grow their military strength, exploit limited resources, expand their territory and choose their strategic approach to exploration, development or conflict.

Cosmos Breakers includes several modes of play: solo or multi, full cooperative mode for up to three players, and a total war mode for direct confrontation. Objectives vary by scenario and explore, secure control of a galactic sector, repel a shared threat, or dominate the galaxy through military supremacy.

—description from the designer
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

description:
The HEL promo card introduces a burst of dark, chaotic energy to the Valhalla expansion. This card channels the power of the Norse goddess of death, shifting the balance of battle at critical moments. HEL’s ability adds a dramatic twist—perfect for players who enjoy high-risk turns, sudden reversals, and unpredictable momentum swings. Distributed exclusively at PAX Unplugged 2025

Card effect: Sacrifice 1 soldier to capture 2 adjacent enemies.

In Deep End, you and your team of marine biologists encounter a ravenous alien squid with the power to twist your psyche and pit you against one another as reality distorts.

To escape, you must outwit the rest of the crew and rise to the surface before madness consumes you.

Deep End is a thrilling, fast-paced shedding card game for 2 to 6 players, ages 8 and up. Dive into a battle of wits where tactics and chaos collide.

Your goal is simple: be the first to shed your cards and warn the world of the horror below or will you let madness consume you and be lost forever in the Deep End?

—description from the publisher

Adds 3 new constructions to help craft goods.


    Windmills lower grain consumption whenmaking bread
    Mines let you dig deeper to find hidden gemstones
    Waterwheels reduce the cost to create barrels

Barnyard Blitz is packed with rapid-fire, hand-slapping chaos as players race to keep up with the mantra “Farmer, Tractor, Chicken, Egg!” On your turn, flip a card onto the central pile while saying the next word in the sequence. If the card matches the word you speak, it’s a Barnyard Blitz, and everyone dives to slap the pile; the fastest player claims all the cards, keeping them in the game longer. Run out of cards and you’re out — last player standing wins!

For even more barnyard bedlam, special action cards mix things up: the Scarecrow makes players freeze in a T-pose, the Harvest sends everyone reaching for the floor before slapping the pile, and the Crow has players squawking as they lunge for the deck. These wild moments keep every round unpredictable, energetic, and irresistibly fun.

—description from the publisher

F**k. The Game is the original and hilarious Aussie card game which will help you get smarter while swearing at your friends.

Hilariously social game – swear at your friends without offending them.
Brain training side effects – you won’t even realise you’re getting smarter.
Laugh at other people’s mistakes – even your smartest friends are bound to f**k up.
Therapeutic benefits – let off steam by innocently shouting profanities.
Perfect pub size – pocket friendly size making it perfect to take to pubs and parties.

Take turns flipping over cards, say the relevant colour or swear word, but don't say Fuck.

Be the first to say all your cards correctly and win.

boardgame: The White War: WW1 on the Alpine Front
description:
While soldiers in France were drowning in mud and the fighting on the Eastern front raged through forests and marshlands, an entirely different kind of war unfolded high in the snow-covered Alps.
The struggle between Austria-Hungary and Italy remains a largely unexplored setting for wargaming. In this rule set, you and an opponent will recreate the brutal alpine clashes along a small stretch of the front between Cortina and Toblach.

This theater was defined by frigid winters and staggering altitudes. Troops on both sides scaled near-vertical rock faces while hauling artillery and supplies, cutting supply paths into sheer cliffs, and digging trenches along exposed ridgelines.

This is not a miniature wargame but rather a board wargame CDG. You will have to print several sheets, the board and the decks of cards for both factions.

I initially made this to be able to game on a diorama map of a small section of the alpine front. At some pointed I decided that I had done enough work that I wanted to be able to share it with the community, so I polished the rules and created all the necessary files to print it and play it.

—description from the designer

boardgame: Rats of Wistar: Mechanical Otter Promo Card
description:
A new special invention card, originally available in 
Cranio's Expansion Pack Kickstarter campaign.

Cost: 1 Underground Tile & 3 Metal
Permanent: Everytime when you play a Wild Token you gain 1 VP
End-game: 0 VPs

SAURUS: Isle of Evolution is a strategic dinosaur survival board game where players lead their species through an ever-changing prehistoric world. Each game begins with a newly built island made of modular terrain tiles, ensuring no two playthroughs feel the same.

Choose your species and evolve them through a mix of exploration, adaptation, territory control, and tactical combat. Discover unique scenarios, encounter rival dinosaurs, gather resources, and outmaneuver other players as the ages pass by.

Adaptation tiles allow your species to grow stronger, smarter, or more specialized, while dynamic events and unique discovery scenarios make every round (age) unpredictable. Whether you’re defending your territory, migrating across the island, or squaring off against the apex species, every decision matters.

In the "Apex Edition", there are over 90 cast pieces, including stunning dinosaur miniatures with 13 dinosaurs per species, terrain figures, and even nests with eggs; SAURUS will bring the prehistoric world to life on your table.

Explore. Expand. Evolve. Survive.
Only the strongest species can claim the Isle of Evolution.

—description from the publisher

Conquest is an Area Control game that is Inspired by COIN AGE.
In this 1-2 players game, you and your friend (or bot) controls different team in the medieval era that are trying to become the greatest leader of the country that has been separated in 2 teams: The (Colour 1) Team VS The (Colour 2) Team.

You need:
8D6 colour 1
8d6 colour 2
8D6 White

boardgame: Scraby: The Pachanga of Hexa-Words Vip
description:
Scraby is a fast-paced, strategic, and social word-building game played on a unique hexagonal honeycomb board.
Players place hex-letter tiles to build words in six possible directions — horizontal, vertical, and four diagonals — unlocking a level of creativity and tactical depth impossible on traditional square grids.

Scraby runs on the UPV Layer System, giving players two modes every turn:

Layer 0 (Chaos Mode): Roll Blue or Red dice for social, unpredictable, and high-energy twists.

Layer 1 (Classic Mode): Skip the roll using tokens and play pure strategy.

Bonus spaces (x2, x3, x4), hex-tile synergies, and layered decision-making turn Scraby into a modern, dynamic, and replayable experience for families, casual players, and word-game lovers.

Fresh, elegant, and re-evolutionary — Scraby reinvents the word-game genre.

—description from the designer

SwaPo (Swap Poker) is a tense, highly interactive card game of negotiation and deception for 2-10 players, using just a standard deck of cards (53 cards including one Joker). You and your opponents aim to collect the most cards of the same rank in your 5-card hand over a series of rounds.

The game’s unique twist is the EXCHANGE PHASE. Each player gets one chance to forcibly exchange cards with any other player. While the exchange is mandatory for the target, players are explicitly encouraged to lie, bluff, and break promises during the negotiation. This is a game where memory and psychology are as crucial as the cards in your hand.

Featuring a simple "set collection" scoring objective, SwaPo builds up tension round after round as players try to manipulate the exchange process and remember which cards are where (counting is allowed). Includes SwaPo Dagger (†) for blind swapping and SwaPo Star (*) for multi-opponent exchanges.

—description from the designer

TOWER: The Pachanga Blocks VIP is an exciting reinvention of classic block-stacking games using the dynamic power of the UPV System — a modular layer of dice, tokens, chaos-control choices, and social interaction.

Players take turns removing blocks and placing them on top, but now every move is amplified by custom red and blue dice, UPV tokens, and unexpected twists that create tension, laughter, and dramatic comebacks.

The Blue Die triggers positive boosts and skill challenges.
The Red Die unleashes chaos, sabotage, and social moments that affect everyone at the table.
Tokens let players block effects, push luck, or force others into tough choices.

Easy to learn but full of strategy, timing, nerve and social energy, TOWER VIP transforms a classic dexterity concept into a highly replayable experience filled with suspense, group interaction, and cinematic game moments.

Part of the UPV Series, a unified design line that reimagines traditional games into modern, fast, social tabletop hits.

—description from the designer

boardgame: Esmeralia: El escape de la isla Emeralda
description:
Will you survive the impact? Or become another story lost among the stars?
The rock is alive. And it won’t be forever.
On a floating island in the middle of space, you live alongside your fellow Esmeralitas, the villagers of Esmeralia—glowing creatures fighting to survive before a meteor wipes everything out.
Your mission: manage vital resources, build your village, and protect your people from cosmic chaos.
Harvest graviplants, extract galacua, care for your space cows… but be careful: solar storms, starborne fungi, droughts, and gravitational anomalies lurk at every turn.

Only those who lead with cunning, cooperate in critical moments, and escape with the most Esmeralitas will be able to claim victory… if anything is left after the impact.

boardgame: マッチングポンプ (Matching Pump)
description:
Players take on the role of factory operators managing a volatile pump system that’s on the verge of blowing! Through careful coordination and timing, players must work together—or against each other—to prevent explosions while maximizing efficiency. Every match card you play could either stabilize the system or push it closer to detonation, so communication and risk management are key to survival.

Matching Pump is a trick-taking game for 3–5 players where each round represents the maintenance of an unstable pump system. Players use numbered “Match Cards” and special “Golden Pump Cards” to influence a shared pump management board. Through play, they must manage the position of a pump marker that can trigger “Explosion” or “Extinguish” effects depending on card combinations. Each trick resolves with careful number comparison and color matching, and players score by avoiding catastrophic chain reactions while timing their “Pump” activations for maximum gain.

In a cozy world inspired by the internet-famous image of cats nestled together in a cooking pot, players gather adorable cats to create the most heartwarming "cat hotpot" imaginable. Each player carefully arranges their cats into the perfect formation, crafting a snug and fluffy masterpiece that embodies both comfort and cuteness.

Mofunabe is a trick-taking and tile-placement game in which players use uniquely shaped cat tiles as their hands. Each round, players simultaneously play one tile, following the suit of cat types and comparing numerical values to determine the trick winner. Winning tricks grants players cat tiles that they can then place onto their personal hotpot board, aiming to fill it efficiently and earn points for completing tidy, full, or harmonious cat arrangements. The combination of strategic tile play and spatial puzzle-solving creates a charming balance of tactics and aesthetics.

boardgame: サイケデリックティキング (Psychedelic Trick-Taking)
description:
High above the clouds, divine birds compete to collect the most dazzling feathers in a radiant contest of beauty and wit. Each player embodies a celestial bird soaring through a kaleidoscopic sky, gathering vibrant feathers in a dazzling spectacle of color and strategy

Psychedelic Trick-Taking is a trick-taking game for 3–5 players where participants aim to collect beautiful feather cards of varying colors and values. Players must follow suit when possible, but the inclusion of rainbow feather cards introduces flexibility and tension in play. Each trick determines not only who wins but also which cards are gained or passed leftward, creating shifting dynamics and tactical trade-offs. Points are scored from collected feathers, with additional bonuses for color sets, and players compete to earn scoring tokens. The first to collect three or more “feather tokens” wins.

boardgame: 実録！ほんとにあった呪いのトリックテイキング (Jitsuroku! Honto ni Atta Noroi no Trick-Taking)
description:
In this eerie game of supernatural suspense, players find themselves as participants in a chilling psychic experiment gone wrong. As unseen forces stir and malevolent curses spread, each decision draws the group closer to doom. Only those who can balance risk, deduction, and courage may survive the mounting fear without succumbing to the curse’s grasp.

実録！ほんとにあった呪いのトリックテイキング
(Jitsuroku! Honto ni Atta Noroi no Trick-Taking, or True Story! The Cursed Trick-Taking Game) is a trick-taking card game for 3–5 players, featuring hidden information, curse accumulation, and timing-based play. Players lead and follow with numbered “Psychic Photograph” cards, attempting to avoid collecting cursed cards while managing color matches and curse triggers. Each round, players reveal and resolve curses based on card values and specific rules, and the player who has accumulated the fewest curses after three rounds is declared the winner.

In a world where civilization has collapsed under the relentless assault of the undead, a small group of survivors fights desperately for one last chance at escape. Working together, they scavenge for supplies, barricade safe zones, and attempt to complete their missions before the ever-growing horde overwhelms them. Every round brings tension and peril, as choices made in cooperation can mean the difference between survival and doom.

No More Tomorrow is a cooperative trick-taking game for 1–4 players in which a team of survivors faces off against relentless zombie decks controlled by the game system. Each round is composed of multiple “tricks,” where players and zombies reveal cards simultaneously following standard must-follow rules. The outcome of each trick determines resource gains, damage received, and progress toward shared and personal objectives.

As danger escalates, players must balance healing, crafting barricades, exploring for supplies, and completing scenario goals before their life or hope runs out. Different chapters and difficulty levels offer a narrative-driven progression, and the game can also be played solo with AI-controlled teammates.

boardgame: 代われそのジョブ (Kaware Sono Job)
description:
Players are members of a peculiar workplace where each person must accomplish specific “job goals,” but after every round, everyone swaps roles entirely—handing off their tasks, tools, and responsibilities to someone else. Success depends on teamwork, coordination, and understanding your colleagues’ work rhythm as you collectively strive to fulfill as many objectives as possible before time runs out.

Kaware Sono Job ("Take Over That Job") is a cooperative trick-taking game for four players played over four rounds. Each player begins with a set of Goal Cards representing how many tricks they aim to win in a round. When a player's actual number of tricks matches one of their goals, that card is discarded as a success. After each round, players pass their entire hand and goals to the next player, forcing everyone to work with the same cards and objectives in rotation.

Gameplay features a “Defensive Stance” system in which only the currently strongest card in a trick remains face up, while weaker cards are turned face down, creating hidden information and tension. Over the course of the game, players also select permanent bonuses—such as setting trump or unfavorable suits—adding layers of strategic coordination to achieve the best collective score.

Worker bees are busy collecting nectar from flowers, unaware that a cunning parasitic cuckoo bee lurks nearby. This deceptive insect seeks the perfect moment to sneak its eggs into the hive, hiding them among the honey and the bees’ own brood. It’s a battle of wit and timing between diligent workers and a stealthy intruder in the world of buzzing hives.

Southern Cuckoo is a short trick-taking game for 2–4 players that uses 16 cards and two types of tokens. Players compete by following suit and playing cards numbered 1–8, where higher numbers win the trick. Each round, players must also manage “species” and “nectar” tokens representing even/odd values and colors—matching these can earn or cost points. After two rounds, the player with the fewest minus points wins.

In a land where fallen warriors refuse to rest until they’ve met their fate on the battlefield, their comrades honor their dying wish: “If I cannot die in battle, I need no grave.” Players oversee the final rites of these unfortunate soldiers, tending to their graves and sending others to their destined wars. Each round becomes a solemn ritual balancing reverence for the dead with the glory of the fight, as players strive to uphold the warriors’ final testament.

Warrior’s Testament is a trick-taking game for 3–5 players in which each round represents the struggle between the call of battle and the final rest of the grave. Players play cards in traditional trick-taking format following a must-follow rule, with blue cards serving as permanent trumps. Certain ranks (4, 6, and 8) trigger special effects that manipulate players’ “graves”, face-down cards representing the fallen, or retrieve them back into hand.

Throughout the three rounds, players must decide whether to commit cards to the battlefield or reserve them for their churchyard. Points are lost through “humiliation points” based on remaining hand cards and grave markers, while glory comes from achieving a perfect warrior’s end — dying with no hand and no graves.
publishers: 卸売センターサンエイ (Oroshiuri Sentaa San'ei)

description:
Players face off in a clever contest of perception and timing, where shifting perspectives and spatial awareness redefine the rhythm of play. Every round brings a dynamic change in orientation, as cards literally rotate on the table, twisting strategies and alliances. It’s a mental duel of anticipation and adaptation as players harness the flow of verticals and horizontals to outmaneuver rivals and claim victory.

Rotate is a trick-taking game for 3–5 players that introduces a unique rotation mechanic: each card can be played in either a vertical or horizontal orientation, showing different numerical values depending on its facing. Players follow suit and compete to win tricks, but specific card interactions can trigger a Rotate condition, causing all cards in play to rotate 90 degrees and change their active values. Special “X” cards add further chaos by instantly flipping the entire field.

Scoring depends on which orientation (vertical or horizontal) dominates, creating a tug-of-war of tactical shifts. The player who best adapts to the ever-changing landscape wins.

In a lively contest of wits and timing, players compete to shed their cards by cleverly connecting numbers and forming powerful combinations. The game’s tension builds as every move can change the flow of play, with players watching each other’s hands and waiting for the perfect chance to link, outplay, and climb to victory in this playful battle of numbers.

Dodicitre is a climbing and shedding game where players aim to play out their cards by either beating the previous set or attaching a card to the one on the table to form a stronger combination. The twist lies in the flexibility of the numbers—cards like 11 and 12 can link or form pairs in unique ways, opening creative paths to victory.

boardgame: The Tricktaker's Guide To The Galaxy
description:
Across the galaxy, countless civilizations have devised their own unique trick-taking games and now, their rules are gathered in one cosmic compendium. Players compete as interstellar editors, wagering precious coins to determine which risky and eccentric rules to include before diving into a single, universal game of galactic trick-taking.

The Tricktaker's Guide To The Galaxy is a trick-taking game for 3–5 players that combines traditional play with an innovative pre-game auction. Before the main game begins, players enter an Editing Phase, where they use coins (which also serve as points) to bid for or avoid strange and risky rule cards that modify gameplay.

Once all rules are assigned, players proceed to a single trick-taking phase using a standard deck of playing cards with must-follow and trump mechanics. Success depends on carefully balancing risk and reward, fulfilling the conditions of the rules you’ve accepted while navigating the shifting chaos of galactic house rules.
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

description:
At a lively karaoke party, players take turns belting out songs and showing off their stage presence. As the night goes on, each performer tries to capture the spotlight by matching the right mood and rhythm, earning applause from their friends. The atmosphere is electric — part singing battle, part social showdown — as everyone competes to become the ultimate karaoke star.

Karaoke Trick is a trick-taking game for 3–5 players where each player competes to win “rounds” of karaoke performances by playing cards that represent different musical tones or performance styles. Each round begins with a “lead” player who sets the “song reservation” (lead suit). Other players must follow suit if possible, and the winner of the trick becomes the next lead.

The game continues for several rounds, and the player with the highest total victory points from their performances is declared the winner. The theme ties closely to karaoke culture, with the suits reflecting different song genres and moods.
publishers: ぷあぷあイデオロギー (Poor Poor Ideology)

description:
In a secretive land of rival ninja clans, warriors gather under the moonlight to prove their mastery of deception and precision. Each ninja conceals their true strength behind technique and illusion, predicting outcomes and risking reputation in a battle of cunning. Every strike, feint, and counter reflects the discipline and danger of life in the shadows, where only the most perceptive shinobi can prevail.

Shinobi Blade is a trick-taking and prediction game for 3–5 players, where participants take on the roles of competing ninja. Each round, players secretly predict their final standing, wagering on their performance before engaging in fast-paced duels of number and color. The game blends traditional trick-taking mechanics with unique “jutsu” (technique) cards that can alter results or negate others’ moves through the special Kiriste Rule, which removes tied plays from consideration.

Strategic foresight is rewarded as players gain or lose points depending on how accurately they forecast their placement across multiple rounds.

Perfect for a birthday celebration, the game centers around a board that lays on top of a plastic molded birthday cake. Players spin a multi-colored spinner to move around the board, collecting different sizes of cake ingredients to put on their own personal cake plate.  The first person to complete their cake by gathering a complete set of five layers in the proper order(cake, filling, cake, frosting, followed by their candle moving piece) wins the game.

Players take on the role of printers preparing for a perfect print job. To reach the “Ready” state, they must balance and collect the four essential inks (Cyan, Magenta, Yellow, and Black). However, too much of any one ink will cause the printer to jam and crash! Through precise calibration and control, players strive to achieve the ideal combination that produces a flawless print.

CMYK Ready is a trick-taking card game where players compete over multiple rounds to collect ink tokens representing the four CMYK colors. Each trick involves comparing the printed “value” of cards, with players able to “calibrate” by flipping cards to alter their values for future plays. The goal is to earn ink tokens without causing a crash by over-collecting. The player who successfully completes their printer’s ink set first or has the most ink at the end wins.

Unique mechanisms like ink calibration, value inversion, and color-based scoring create a clever puzzle of timing and precision.

Deal 5 letter cards to each player.
Each turn, a player draws a card with a theme on it. Each player has to quickly find word for the chosen theme starting with a letter from his card: the first 3 words enable players to discard their card and progress towards victory.

A rare board game, manufactured and released only in Greece in the late 60s / early 70s by Remoundo, based on the famous tv series Mission Impossible.

The game consists of 8 pawns, 4 of the same color for one team and 4 of the same color for the other, 2 pawns of a different type (1 for each team), and 2 dice.

Each player places the 4 pawns of the same color in their headquarters. The player with the highest number starts. The different types of pieces are brought into play and placed permanently in the green circle on the board only when a player rolls a total of 9 on the dice. Players move their pieces in any direction and along any lane they wish.

In order for a player to move their pieces onto their opponent's territory, there are only two points on the board and the player is required to move their piece or pieces to those specific points and follow the instructions in the instruction booklet.

The winner is the player who places at least two pieces in the opponent's headquarters.

Red Dungeon is a strategic print & play game for 1–4 players, based on:

     tactical decisions and risk
     action board combinations
     cyclical Light and Dark rounds
     high-stake special actions
     economic resource management
     dynamic Events and Conspiracy cards


You lead a rebellion in the depths of a cursed mine, fighting for gold, crystals, keys and freedom against relentless Guards.

1. Print & Play mechanic based on a poker cards
Each player uses a standard 52-card deck to perform actions, generate resources, or fight the control system.
2. Strong sense of progression and time pressure
The Dark and Light cycle affects difficulty and Guard behavior.
3. Exciting risk and rewards
Special actions can grant huge bonuses… or cost the entire turn.
4. Tense narrative
Gather resources, build machines, sabotage, bribe Guards, and prepare a great escape.
5. High replayability
Random events, unique Leaders, varied Maps and Conspiracy cards

—description from the designer

boardgame: Gévaudan, sur les traces de la Bête
description:
Each turn, players analyse the Footprint tokens to determine if the Beast of Gévaudan has passed through one of the four terrain categories: River, City, Forest, or Dragon Patrol.
The clues gathered allow them to confirm or eliminate terrain spaces to gradually locate its lair.
The first player to place their trap on the exact spot wins the King's bounty… unless the Beast is the ultimate winner.

The game offers a Standard mode, ideal for discovering the mechanics and getting started with the game, and a Historical mode, for experiencing the full Gévaudan hunt.

Between 1764 and 1767, the Gévaudan region was the scene of a series of mysterious attacks attributed to a ferocious creature, larger and faster than a wolf. Despite the hunts led by peasants, nobles, and the royal army, the Beast long eluded its hunters. From Marie-Jeanne Vallet to Jean Chastel, several figures claimed responsibility for its killing, without ever fully revealing its origins.
Even today, the mystery remains: a fearsome animal, a human conspiracy, or a legend born of fear?

The thinking pirate's game of deduction and treasure!

Hide your plunder, hunt theirs down. Two clever captains clash through questions and logic - only the sharpest wins!

Takin' Treasures is a 2-player logical deduction game where you race to discover your opponent's buried treasures before they find yours. Ask strategic questions, track the answers on your Treasure Tracker, and use logic to discover their treasures. Simple to learn, endlessly replayable, and plays in just 10 minutes.

boardgame: Kursk in Finland in 1944: Operation Tali-Ihantala
description:
Very few games at the game scale exist in this 300+ AFV battle that resembled the Kursk battle in 1943. 
In June, 1944, the Russians launched a massive tank assault in Finland hoping to take control of key cities deep in the country. The Finnish Army was already weakened and pushed back when this began and with not more than 60 StG assault guns, including the Germans, this battle was a David vs. Goliath scenario that was hard to imagine a Finnish victory. But, what the Finnish Army lacked, geography would be a key factor in this battle lasting a week. The daunting number of tanks employed by the Russian was no less than massive.

The game has a 11x17 inch map, over 240 counters. 
This is a print and play game.

—description from the publisher

Split the numbers, match the colors! With only 18 cards, 2-4 players build a shared lattice of bi-color numbered cards by playing cards perpendicular to each other and gaining points from placing numbers on the short ends of cards within ranges of numbers on the long edge of cards. Score bigger points for tighter ranges, and numbers with fewest placement possibilities. Keep tally of your points each round to track how well you are doing. Feel the excitement of landing big points with the perfect placement!

—description from the publisher

Tempo consists of the board and 40 pieces, 28 of which represent the common soldiers and 12 the officers. The game is played by two opponents, each receiving 14 soldiers and, for any promotions, 6 officers. The two opponents place their 14 soldiers in the last three ranks, on the positions marked by circles.
The soldiers move along the prescribed paths to the right, left, and straight ahead. The same applies to the diagonal paths of the trenches.If a soldier manages to advance into the last enemy battle line, he is promoted to officer and differs from the soldiers in that he is allowed to move backward. However, he loses his rank and the right to move backward as soon as he has lost all his troops and comrades.
A soldier is captured when the opponent succeeds in blocking his path from all three sides. In this case, the captured soldier is removed from the board. Since retreat to the Masurian Lakes as well as to the neutral territories is impossible, the squares located on them are considered blocked from one side. 
The capture of an officer, who can also move backwards, therefore occurs when he is also blocked from a fourth side. The capture of several soldiers or officers is done in the same way as that of a single one, namely by blocking from three or two sides, respectively.
The ultimate goal of the game is to capture all enemy soldiers or the enemy fortress. The latter is achieved when at least three entrances to the fortress are occupied.
If both opponents are left with two stones each, regardless of whether they are officers or soldiers, the game remains a draw unless a decision can be reached in 30 moves.

—user summary
family: Players: Wargames with Rules Supporting Only Two Players

description:
Muto is a 2-player climbing and shedding game. Each round, players receive 14 cards from a 39-card deck. The dealer starts by playing any legal combination: singles, suited runs, pairs, or triplets.

On each turn, the opponent must either overplay the combination or pass. Overplays can follow normal climbing rules (same type and length, higher rank), but single-suited combinations have an additional “suit-based overplay” rule: spades can be beaten by any 1-card combo, hearts by any 2-card combo, and clubs by any 3-card combo, even with lower ranks.

The round ends when someone empties their hand; that player earns 1 point. The first player to reach 3 points wins the game.

—description from the designer

The Khalifa Vibe + Chill Party Game is the party game that fits any mood. Two games in one, so whether you’re super chill or just vibing out, this is the game for you and your squad. Choose which mood you and your friends are in and roll with it…

VIBE is the game to choose when the crew is hyped and wants to get on the same wavelength. Take turns secretly ranking 5 different topics, then sit back and laugh while the rest of the group collaboratively tries to figure out your rankings. Get more right than wrong and the group wins.

CHILL is The perfect game for when everyone is just sitting around and being mellow. Ask one of the questions to the person in the group who you think will say “YES!”. Learn more about each other and prove who knows their friends the best. Win by getting rid of your hand of cards.

—description from the publisher

Vicinity is a solo tile placement game. You're building a city and need to fill it with enough houses. Each house has a number of requirements it needs to have in its vicinity, so you need to place your tiles carefully to reach your goal.

—description from the designer

boardgame: A Quest for Dragons: The Expansion
description: This ten-card expansion adds extra dragons, coins, action cards, and recruitable heroes to allow for a fifth and sixth player.

FlipSide 10 is a fast, lightweight press-your-luck game where every toss of the custom die brings players closer to victory—or sends them tumbling back to the start. Players gain points, lose points, or even target others depending on the face they reveal, creating an exciting race to hit exactly 10 without overshooting. Go over, and you flip back down to 5, turning even a strong lead into a sudden scramble to recover.

Each side of the die creates meaningful choices: steady gains, big jumps, point swaps with other players, or powerful one-time Blank bonuses that can swing the game at the perfect moment. With every toss called out before revealing, the tension builds as players commit to bold risks or careful climbs.

In multiplayer, players toss, catch, and reveal effects in turn, choosing whom to target and when to use the once-per-game Hold—a defensive move that ignores the revealed result. In solo mode, players push for precision using limited Blank uses and must avoid dropping the die or overshooting to stay in the game.

Easy to teach, quick to play, and packed with dramatic swings, FlipSide 10 delivers a burst of excitement in just a few minutes. Whether you’re racing friends around a table, practicing precision in solo mode, or playing with younger gamers learning numbers and probability, FlipSide 10 is a snappy push-to-10 challenge that keeps everyone engaged from the first toss to the last.'

—description from the designer

Al-Kahuf is an adventure detective game inspired by the works of Howard Phillips Lovecraft.

You’ll experience an engaging interactive story whose outcome depends entirely on you and your choices. You are about to set out in the footsteps of a missing expedition that had been exploring the area near the town of Al-Kahuf before vanishing in the scorching sands of Egypt. Talk to characters, explore locations, carry out excavations. The outcome of the story will depend on your actions, choices, and deductions.

The game features a "nonlinear communication" mechanic, allowing the player to interact with various in-game characters on different topics at their discretion.

—description from the designer

An epic war game on the siege of Jerusalem in 70AD.  It is a reimagining/updating/rebuild/new design of the classic Avalon Hill game.

As the Roman player you are using all of your skills and discipline to overwhelm the walls and defenders of Jerusalem.

As the Jewish player you will have to overcome a lack of training and a fragmented defense to make the Roman's pay a price so high in blood that they give up the assault.

Playing time 3 to 8 hours.
Game comes with 3 to 5 counter sheets with beautiful double sided counters with rounded corners.
A hard mounted 34" x 44" game board drawn by Jose Faura.

—description from the publisher

In Rink Rumble, 2 players go head-to-head in a tug of war on the ice, emulating an ice-hockey game. Players will fight for control of the puck, play cards, and roll a die to determine the outcome of a play.

The first player to score 3 goals wins the game.

—description from the designer

boardgame: From the Other Side: Welcome to Dying
description:
The dungeons of Dragon Rock hide a bitter surprise for the Blind Guardian heroes: Phylax the Dragon ...and he's not alone!

The witches’ coven has revealed that one of his three offspring could emerge from the well: Khion the Dragon of Ice, Pyros the Dragon of Fire, and Zophos the Dragon of Darkness. Ruthless and terrible because they were born in captivity and raised in the resentment and frustration of their father, these Dragons have terrible powers that the Heroes will discover when the Dragons lash out against them!

Welcome to Dying features a miniature dragon that can be assembled with magnets and replaces the Dragon board from the base game, but its operation remains the same: first the head comes out, then the torso with the head, and when the Dragon has completely emerged, the players lose the game.

In addition, Welcome to Dying includes 5 cards for each of Phylax’s sons and a new Hero card for each Hero, which are added to their base decks.

—description from the publisher

All you have to do is guess which of the five questions your friends answered.

Your friend answered “7.” Is that the number of pieces of toilet paper they use when they poop? Or their ideal number of children?

The perfect icebreaker for playing in large groups, whether with new or old friends: you're sure to learn something new about the people you're playing with!

—description from the publisher (translated)

Todo lo que tienes que hacer es adivinar cuál de las cinco preguntas han contestado tus amigos.

Tu amigo ha respondido “7”. ¿Es el número de trozos de papel higiénico que utilizan cuando hacen caca? ¿O su número ideal de hijos?

El rompehielos perfecto para jugar en grupos grandes, ya sean nuevos o viejos amigos: ¡seguro que aprendes algo nuevo sobre la gente con la que juegas!

—description from the publisher

A meteoroid has struck the space station and left tools, data samples, and parts floating nearby. Complete 3 missions to retrieve the items and station fragments so the puffins can repair the station and make it home.

Pocket Puffins: LiS is a solo only, 18 card game that uses grid movement and pattern building to complete missions. Players take the role of commander and move puffins in a grid to match the pattern on designated cards. Players must be swift with their commands because all missions must be completed before time runs out.

Using another dimension than the original Pocket Puffins, Lost in Space is a challenging puzzle with adjustable difficulty and a 10 minute playtime.

—description from the designer

This complete set includes a Plinko board with sound, 20 chips for nonstop fun. Enjoy interactive features like optional lights and sound effects to enhance every drop, or play without them for a quieter experience. Built with durable, all-weather construction, it's perfect for both indoor and outdoor use. The foldable design and built-in chip storage make it easy to pack up and take anywhere. Plus, the set includes digital game cards with over 100 products to price, delivering the full Price is Right experience for game nights, parties, and family gatherings.

—description from the publisher

The hilarious, creative and clever family game where all lies are accepted!

Who will be the best bluffers in the family?

The rules are super simple: Read a question with your team, one player will tell the truth, the other players on the team will give a fake answer and the opposing team will have to guess the correct answer.
Discover 240 unusual "Questions for a Pigeon"! Example: England organizes the world competition for...? (Answer: Grimaces!)

Bet your chips on the answer you and your team think is correct. 
Beware: if you're wrong, you lose your chips! The team that manages to keep all its bets wins.

—description from the publisher

The Kelpie is a legendary horse of Scottish folklore.
 It also has connections to some areas of Yorkshire, including the Strid near Bolton Abbey.

This tabletop miniatures game takes inspiration from the mythological horse by having one player take control of the Kelpie, trying to drown victims in the river, and the other player embodying a group of Villagers trying to cross with their goods.

The Villagers can cross the river unmolested until they fail a River test. At which point, the Kelpie is summoned and attempts to drag them to their doom.

Kelpie is a 2-page skirmish game of risk and reward that plays in under 30 minutes.

What You Need:

     Models - 9 models to represent the Villagers. 1 Horse model for the Kelpie
     Measuring - A tape measure or stick
     Dice - A handful of D6
     Play Area - A 12” x 12” space
     Terrain - The River itself should be 12” wide. 
     Goods - Tokens or Dice to represent the Villagers' goods
     Time - 20-30 minutes


—description from the publisher
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups
family: Players: Wargames with Rules Supporting Only Two Players

description:
The Kelpie is a legendary horse of Scottish folklore.
 It also has connections to some areas of Yorkshire, including the Strid near Bolton Abbey.

This tabletop miniatures game takes inspiration from the mythological horse trying to drown a group of Villagers crossing a river. This is the solo version of this game, so only the Villagers are playable. There is also a two-player version.

The Villagers attempt to cross the river unmolested until they fail a River test. At which point, the Kelpie is summoned and attempts to drag them to their doom.

Kelpie is a 2-page skirmish game of risk and reward, and it plays in under 30 minutes.

What You Need:

     Models - 9 models to represent the Villagers. 1 Horse model for the Kelpie
     Measuring - A tape measure or stick
     Dice - A handful of D6
     Play Area - A 12” x 12” space
     Terrain - The River itself should be 12” wide. 
     Goods - Tokens or Dice to represent the Villagers' goods
     Time - 20-30 minutes


—description from the publisher

In a world ruled by colorful bear gangs, each group vies for dominance by outsmarting and outmaneuvering their rivals. The bears compete fiercely to establish control, playing their cards with cunning timing and strategic wit. Only the sharpest and most adaptable gang of bears can outlast the chaos and emerge as the top crew in this fast-paced turf battle.

CGOB (Color Gang Out Bear) is a climbing-style card game where players aim to be the first to play all of their cards. Players must strategically manage their hands, playing combinations based on color and numerical order while navigating the restrictions of the shared “path” and “field.” Timing when to reset the table or pass can mean the difference between victory and defeat. Simple rules hide deep tactical layers, rewarding clever sequencing and sharp observation.

Skybreaker: Genesis is a hidden-role board game for 3 to 8 players where heroes battle for the supremacy of their faction. Players control one of 12 unique Heroes, each possessing a special ability, but their true allegiance remains hidden at the start of the game.

Players are secretly assigned to one of three factions:


     Caelis Dorma: The kingdom of light, whose objective is to defeat all Nacht heroes.
     Nacht: The kingdom of darkness, whose objective is to defeat all Caelis Dorma heroes.
     Junos: An independent, neutral kingdom. Junos players have variable winning conditions ranging from "Survival" (being the last one alive) to "Chaos" (winning if no other faction wins) or simply collecting specific items. They may choose to ally with a side or play only for themselves.


—description from the designer

Tiny Robot is a card based tabletop game for 2-4 players. Uniquely, we think, a production line/ game board is created at random at the start of every game, by shuffling and laying one card from the pack face up and four cards face down in a line, in the centre of the table, with a winner card placed at the end. Players must complete sets in order to travel along the production line, whilst collecting and building robots along the way. To win the game players must reach the winner card at the end of the production line, and have collected the most robot cards along the way.

The pack is made up of factory cards and action cards. Factory cards, including robot cards, are not only used to build sets and aid your progression along the production line, but can also be used to build robots utilising various components. The action cards are used to aid your progression and hinder your opponents. examples of action cards include pinching robots, swapping cards, extra cards, jumping opponents, extending the production line, moving yourself and opponents backwards and forwards etc.

The games success comes from the autonomy each player has with the cards they are dealt, and the fact every game is different because of the way the production line/ board is laid out.

It is portable, packing neatly into a durable little box no bigger than a bar of soap. Very easy to learn and very addictive. Players can play the game in their own style, be it combative, collaborative or just head down minding their own business. The game is both strategic and tactical and above all really good fun.

—description from the designer

boardgame: Golden Journal Number 60: 9 April 1940 – Germany Invades Denmark
family: Series: Avalanche Press Gold Club Golden Journal Supplements

description:
Based on our popular board game Bag of Dungeon, Book of Dungeon is part puzzle book, part fantasy adventure.

All you need is a pencil, a pair of dice and your courage, draw and explore 100 deadly dungeons. Slay monsters, dodge traps and hunt for treasure. Each dungeon is a self-contained puzzle: draw your path, make your choices, and see if you can escape with your life.

—description from the publisher
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

description:
In Sengoku: Clash of the Daimyō, players lead rival warlords struggling to unify Japan during the Sengoku period. On a shared grid-based map inspired by Sudoku, you deploy troops, build castles and capitals, forge uneasy alliances – and deliberately create “errors” on the board only to turn them into brutal tactical advantages.

Each player controls a historical Daimyō with a unique deck, starting power and personal mission. During the game you play cards to claim rows, columns and regions on the map, place tokens, and influence key locations such as castles, capitals and markets. Formal alliances let you cooperate with other players… at least for as long as it suits your plans.

The core twist of Sengoku is its “error and correction” system: sometimes you intentionally break the rules of the Sudoku-like grid to gain a short-term benefit, leaving behind fragile “errors” that can later be punished, corrected or even weaponised by clever opponents. Timing when to create, protect or exploit these errors is at the heart of the game.

—description from the designer

Outbound is an asymmetric strategy game played on a Goban with Go stones. In this game, there are no draws, no ties, no cycles, no luck, no randomness, and no hidden information.

To set up the game, Black begins with a stone in the center, and a crown token. The king is the Black group containing the crown. White can start from a few standard setups, or players can take turns placing White stones anywhere along the perimeter until one player, instead of placing a stone, declares they can stop Black with the current White setup, and makes the first move as White.

Each White turn, White moves one piece in a straight line, orthogonal or diagonally, over empty or friendly spaces , onto an empty space. If such a movement results in two white pieces flanking a single black piece, orthogonally or diagonally, the black piece is flipped to white. A single move can result in multiple flips. However, the black piece containing the crown can never be flipped.

Each Black turn, Black may grow each of their groups (those with liberties) once, by placing a stone orthogonally adjacent to a stone in that group. A group may only grow once per turn; in other words, each placement consumes all the potential for growth for all the groups it was placed adjacent to, for the remainder of the turn. However, growing a different un-grown group may lead to merging of previously-grown black groups. At the end of Black's turn, the crown moves to the most recently-placed stone in the king.

Black wins if the king reaches the edge. White wins if the king cannot grow because it lacks liberties.

Outbound was designed by Alek Erickson in November 2025. Michael Amundsen and Samraku provided useful suggestions during the design process.

—description from the designer

Vaemora is a science fiction tabletop experience where hunters face off against powerful alien monsters in a fully asymmetric contest of strategy and survival. One player controls a monster with unique abilities, evolving combat styles, and a growing deck of advanced actions, while the remaining players form a coordinated hunter team using specialised gear and tactical roles.

The game unfolds on one of two maps, where positioning matters as much as the cards in your hand. Hunters work together to trap, weaken, and outmanoeuvre the creature, using teamwork to bring its health to zero before it overwhelms them. The monster fights back using its own deck choices, environmental advantages, and brute force, aiming to eliminate the entire hunter squad.

Each session offers a mix of deck building, tactical movement, and high-pressure combat decisions. Players can customise their characters or creature through upgrade paths, allowing every match to feel distinct and giving both sides new tools and strategies to explore.

Victory is simple but intense. The hunters win if they defeat the monster, and the monster wins if it defeats the hunters. Whether you stand with a team or take control of the creature, Vaemora delivers a tense and cinematic encounter in every play.

—description from the publisher

Brassbound: Adamantine Dawn is a two-player, tabletop war game set in a dieselpunk post-apocalyptic setting. Towering Brassbounders lead legions of soldiers and tanks across ruined battlefields to secure precious Adamantine reserves.
