Play as the high-tech Coalition, the pragmatic Pact, or create your own unique faction with their own identity and backstory. Using the Unit Creator, you can bring your own models and units to life!

Getting started is easy! Learn the core rules in 15 minutes by playing the Quick Start Scenario. Follow the step-by-step instructions to craft your armies, choosing from 16 different models. You can use whatever loose bricks you have or buy the models from us directly.

Craft a dynamic battlefield rich with Strategic Points to contest, cover to hide behind, and terrain to create interesting choke points. Are you fighting in the ruins of a city pillaged by the Jötunn, a burned-out forest, or over an industrial wasteland? You get to decide!

—description from the designer

You hear the signature click, clack of a train entering your valley. This is a prime opportunity that you cannot let slip away. So, you whistle for your horse and shoot off towards the billow of steam flowing from the top of the train. This robbery will be your biggest haul yet.

Welcome to Full Steam Ahead, a solo or multiplayer game of bandits robbing a train. 
Each player takes control of a bandit and their horse, and must carefully use a hand of standard playing cards to leap aboard the train, rob its passengers and jump back off again into the sunset.

Every card you use goes into your discard, and if you have no cards left, one is permanently lost for the rest of the game. Once aboard the train, players play a pseudo hand of blackjack to determine if they successfully steal anything. 
Succeed and the score is yours, fail and you are thrown from the train chasing its dust to try your luck again.

What You Need

     Models -1 Horse and Rider, a train with 3 carriages (or something to represent the train)
     Playing Cards - Two decks of standard playing cards
     Terrain - An outback or desert scene with railway tracks. A central bandit hideout for the middle of the board
     Play Area - 32” x 32”
     Playtime - approximately 30 minutes (longer with more players)


Turn Structure:
If Full Steam Ahead is being played solo, then the
player takes 2 actions and then the train moves.
If Full Steam Ahead is being played by two or more
players, then all the players take a single action, and
then the train moves.

MANIPULATION - ALLIANCES - DECEPTION.

The friendliest treason game!

The enigmatic Cuckoo Island, lost in the middle of the Pacific Ocean, is ruled by the 7 members of the Council of the Wise.
As evil spirits of the Island, you must tighten your grip and the influence you hold over the Council members in order to accomplish your darkest plans. Beware, for you cannot act alone... nor directly...

MAD is a game of influence, alliances and treason. Players try to influence 3 council members but need to do so through other players' actions.

On a turn, a player chooses an Influence card and gives it to another player. They order which Council Member should receive that card. If that player agrees, they place the card face-down on that character... or they can place it on any other character face-up. 2 rounds, 4 turns per round. You need to find your allies as quickly as possible if you want to win.

—description from the publisher

boardgame: Rust and Revenue: Historic Map Pack #1
description:
Historic Map Pack #1 expands Rust & Revenue with four new real-world inspired maps. Each one preserves the core flow of the base game while introducing fresh shapes, new share considerations, and regional quirks that shift tempo and strategy. The Southeast USA map adds natural choke points around Atlanta, the Maine map explores tight coastal builds, the UK/Ireland map rewards long arcs, and the Germany map focuses on dense central pressure. The pack is designed to be modular: new players can play the maps as is, while experienced players can layer in company powers and variants for more challenge.

—description from the designer

You are a vampire managing a tableau of human thralls. Each thrall provides powerful abilities while alive. But you must devour them for blood to survive the night.

Every turn you choose: Play a thrall for future power or devour one for blood. 16 turns to survive, 4 escalating blood requirements. As you devour thralls, you collect bond symbols that unlock vampire abilities: Spades for explosive blood generation, Hearts for suspicion management, Diamonds for card advantage.

Blood Economy is designed under a 36-card constraint: 30 thralls, 5 vampires, 1 reference/tracker. No dice, no boards, no tokens beyond what you have at home to track turns and resources.

Each vampire transforms the strategy completely: the Elder rewards balanced play, the Beast demands aggressive feeding, the Socialite focuses on preservation, and others. With 30 scenarios printed on card backs (ranging from beginner tutorials to expert challenges), you get 150 unique combinations.

—description from the designer

boardgame: Valheim: The Board Game – Haldor's Hoard
description:
Was not available as an add-on but was only an exclusive expansion to the Gamefound's Collector Edition and All-in pledges. Came in its own box.

The custom metal coins can replace the cardboard coin from Standard edition and the wooden coins from Deluxe edition.

The two Odin Gear Cards are game play additions to the game.
All other items are cosmetic upgrades.

Content of the box:
- Haldor's Camp miniature
- 2 Exclusive Odin Gear cards
- 12 Holographic cards to replace the Haldor gear cards
- 7 metal coins
- chest miniature

boardgame: Manatee Sanctuary: The Green Manatee Mini Expansion
description:
The Green Manatee Mini Expansion introduces 15 Green Manatees that change the base game with 3 different independent abilities for added complexity.

Includes:
15 Green Manatees 
Double sided rule card
Double sided ability card

Welcome to Rising Fear, the newest edition of the hit board game Ring of Chaos from Beadle & Grimm’s. Rising Fear takes all the competitive fun of the original edition and relocates it to a chilling new setting full of haunted graveyards, animated corpses, and fiendish grave robbers. This horror-themed new version of Ring of Chaos is a stand-alone game that does not require the original set to play, so it’s perfect for newcomers and experienced fans alike.

For savvy Ring of Chaos veterans, Rising Fear offers several twists to the rules that create new surprises and challenges, along with two terrifying maps with unique rules of their own. Everything in this new set is also compatible with the original game, so you can mix new parties and old parties, or play the parties from one set on the maps from the other! With six new monstrous Parties all bringing their special powers into the mix, every game of Ring of Chaos: Rising Fear will feel fresh, new and utterly horrifying!

—description from the publisher

Embark on an introspective journey to troubleshoot your damaged synthetic friend from within. Unsettled: LUNA is the 12th planet box for Unsettled.

Get under your stalwart companion’s hard outer-shell on an emotional quest to repair her from the inside. Tread thoughtfully though, the damage may go deeper than her circuits. Unique movement mechanics and a programming line that represents your interactions with LUNA’s internal systems set the stage for heartfelt moments and harrowing finishes.

Space is hard. Robot CPR is harder.

—description from the publisher

boardgame: Warhammer Age of Sigmar: Path to Glory – Blighted Wilds
description:
The Plague God Nurgle has long sought to steal Ghyran from Alarielle the Everqueen, bringing the verdant Realm of Life into his vile and bounteous garden. In Path to Glory: Blighted Wilds you can join this seemingly never-ending battle for Ghyran to either help rescue Thyria from the clutches of the Grandfather, or bring it to ruin for your own nefarious reasons.

This 90-page narrative expansion for Warhammer Age of Sigmar contains a new battlepack, Blighted Wilds, with rules for playing a campaign with unique abilities, rewards, battle scars, battleplans, two Paths for heroes, two Paths for units, universal enhancements, plus spell and prayer lores.

There are also rules for building your own Landmark of Ghyran, a powerful faction terrain piece with abilities of your choosing, and creating your own Regiment of Ghyran, a personalised, custom Regiment of Renown that you can give a variety of upgrades and idiosyncrasies. Finally, there are 12 narrative battleplans in the book, and a battleplan map so you can link them together as you play through a campaign to befoul or purify Thyria.

—description from the publisher

Debt-Fueled Rivalry at Its Most Chaotic

In Line Go Up!, players build towering pyramids of leveraged debt in a fast-paced, tongue-in-cheek economic showdown. Each turn, you take out new loans, reinvest them into ever-riskier financial “structures,” and stack your debt higher and higher—because if "your line isn’t going up", you’re losing.

But beware: every bit of borrowed capital pushes you closer to collapse. Your rivals are playing the same dangerous game, eyeing the moment your overextended pyramid buckles so they can profit from the fallout. Time your expansions, hedge your risks, and try to trigger their bubble before yours bursts.

It’s a game of brinkmanship, bluffing, and hilarious financial hubris where only one thing matters: Keep your line going up…
and make sure someone else pops first.

—description from the publisher

In Dreadquarters, 1-4 players take on the roles of supervillains who have been “strongly encouraged” to cooperate while establishing a new shared evil lair. Each villain brings their own unique deck of cards, playstyle, minions, and objective — from building doomsday devices to harvesting souls and hatching insect swarms. All while working together to defend your lair from waves of do-gooders who aim to destroy it.

Each round, you expand your wing of the lair with bizarre rooms, reinforce them with over-engineered upgrades, and deploy your minions to work stations, city raids, or desperate last stands. When the heroes arrive, your careful machine kicks into motion: traps trigger, combat erupts, plans collide, and everything either works beautifully… or collapses spectacularly.

Dreadquarters combines puzzle-like tactical play with the subtle comedy of villains sharing limited space. Every card you build affects the entire team, every minion placement matters, and every incoming champion threatens to unravel the entire operation. With dozens of villains, cities, and scenario combinations, no two lairs will ever look — or explode — the same.

—description from the designer

A wise man once said, “If you and your friends meet a bear in the woods, you don’t have to outrun the bear—you just have to outrun your friends.”

In this fast, fun, and competitive dice game, you’ll discover things far worse than bears! Roll to collect Steps, grab Items, or trigger Specials that shake up the chase. Keep rolling until the Items are gone—then see who wasn’t quick enough to escape to the next round.

But beware: anyone who falls behind doesn’t just lose… they switch sides! The fallen return as part of the undead horde, alien mind, or skeleton army—bent on taking everyone else down.

Outrunning your friends might not be easy… but it’s guaranteed to be a whole lot of fun!

—description from the designer

A new set of question cards for SMART10! This expansion includes 50 double-sided cards featuring 100 brand-new questions and a total of 1,000 answers all about the exciting world of video games.
With Smart10 Video Games you’ll put your knowledge of digital gaming to the test: from retro classics to the latest releases, including development studios, iconic characters, and much more.
You can replace the original question cards in the SMART box with two themed expansion packs, or mix these video-game questions with half of the original set. To play, the SMART box included in the Smart10 base game is required.

You head into the dungeon he created in search of the treasure that lies deep within. This dungeon is cursed with a curse that causes rifts between companions, and it is said that this curse has wiped out many famous adventurer parties... This is why you decide to enter the dungeon alone. Will you be able to obtain even more treasure in this dungeon?


     Advance through the dungeon by choosing between two options!
     HP, hunger, attack power, defense power... Use various numbers and items to reach the lower levels!
     Defeat the Lone King and bring back even more treasure!


—description from the publisher

Try your luck, choose your upgrades, and line up your Patitos en Fila (Ducklings in a Row). Don't forget to collect coins!

Watch out! If the toads appear... CRACKLE! and your turn ends.

Take risks on each turn and choose whether to score points or collect coins.

Power up your upgrade tree to get more points.

Each card reveals a classic element of horror. Your mission: summon a work that contains it.

Cemeteries, rituals, blades, and masks... Do you have enough obsession to survive this round?

Test your knowledge of horror. In Clichés del Terror, you must quickly answer on your turn by naming, without repeating, a horror work that contains at least one of the clichés from the active cards of the round.

At some random moment, the balloon will pop, and whoever's turn it is will lose one of their lives.

Take the fast S-Bahn to catch up with the Böögg and accompany him to the most beautiful destinations. But be careful: the other players will try to snatch the Böögg away from you again. The first player to lead the Böögg to their own destinations wins the game.

All players have hidden destination cards, which correspond to places on the map. On their turn, players roll the dice to reach the Böögg figure. Once they reach it, they then move with the Böögg until another player steals it. As soon as a player playing with the Böögg reaches one of their hidden destinations, they can discard their card. The first player to reach all their destinations with the Böögg and discard all four cards wins the game.

Toots and Poops: The Dice Rolling Game That's a Real Gas!

Get ready to roll, giggle, and maybe hold your nose—Toots and Poops is the hilariously gassy dice game where strategy meets flatulence! Perfect for parties, family game night, or anyone who isn’t afraid to laugh at a little stink.

Each player takes turns rolling the custom "Toot & Poop" dice, aiming to collect the most Toots before they trigger a Poop Explosion and lose their turn! Special action cards can save your butt—or sabotage your friends’. But be warned: one wrong roll, and things could get messy (figuratively, of course)!

—description from the publisher

Adds three exciting previously released modules:

The Executives – Choose from 13 unique player powers to shape your strategy.

The Moguls – Recruit industry legends with game-breaking abilities.

Telly Time – Take over the UK in a new game mode featuring unique genre tracks, extra time slots, and more.

Note: This expansion is only compatible with The Networks: Primetime edition.

boardgame: Paranoia: Five Clones from Alpha Complex
description:
The miniatures version of the iconic, dark-humour Paranoia RPG, adapted by the same team as Five Parsecs from Home and Five Leagues from the Borderlands.

Want a game where teammates have each other’s backs and work together to accomplish a noble goal? Well, this ain’t it.

Join a so-called ‘team’ of Troubleshooters (find the trouble, get shot) and complete missions that keep Alpha Complex the safe, efficient Utopia its citizens trust. None of them are members of secret societies, and absolutely none of them have mutant powers. They’re certainly not going to report each other as Traitors or shoot each other in the back with a laser pistol. Hey, get them before they get you!

And if you die, don’t worry, you have five identical clones to take your place and mop up what’s left of you.

Trust The Computer. It never makes mjstakes.

Procedural solo, co-op and group play rules and gamemastered options throughout.
Fast-moving system, emphasising fun over complexity
50 Mutant Powers that you definitely don’t have
20 pieces of starting equipment.
Mission generator, plus 20 Scenarios with 7 Objective types.
Secret Society and Security Clearance rules
Foes including IntSec Troopers, Vulture Squadron Warriors, Bots, Mutant Creatures, Monsters, and Aliens, and, of course, your fellow clones
Rules for random hazards, cameras, vending machines, even doors
Optional rules for Mandatory Bonus Duties, Service Groups, and more
10 status effects – you too can be Confused, Stunned, Bleeding, Stressed, Infected, and more. Often at the same time!


Embrace the anxiety.
Paranoia will keep you safe.
Trust No-One and Keep Your Laser Handy!

—description from the publisher

Stackup is a two player abstract strategy game played on a hexagonal grid board. One player uses white pieces and the other uses black. Players place and stack pieces to form a multi level structure, aiming to have the majority of their color visible on the highest completed level at the end of the game.

Each player has pieces in two different lengths, divided into four types: basic (18), blocker (4), neutral (2), and traitor (2). At the start of a players turn the player places one piece so that it covers fields on a single level. Pieces may be placed directly on the base or on top of existing pieces, provided they follow the placement rules for their type. If a player can’t or chooses not to make a move on his turn, he must discard a piece from the game.

A new level may only be created when a piece is placed so that it bridges one white field and one black field.

When a piece is placed across two fields of the players own colour, he earns an extra move. That extra move must start on the piece placed but cannot sit fully on top of it.

Players alternate turns until both have placed (or discarded) all of their pieces, ending the game. The highest level is then scored. The player with the most fields of their color visible on that level wins. If that level is tied, the next level down is scored, and so on, until a winner is determined.

—description from the designer

boardgame: Cavern Shuffle: Peril on the Planet
description:
The Premise:Based on the classic Solitaire, Cavern Shuffle is a solo-player dungeon delver game where the player must level up their party in order to uncover what lies ahead of them. Using familiar mechanics, players will uncover useful Items, overcome Obstacles and defeat the Boss Encounter to win!

				
				
					The Setting:Something has gone awry on the surface of this newly terraformed planet. A new infection has developed and has begun spreading itself across the grove. In this adventure we will meet the Crawler, a mech harvester that has been deployed to survey and protect this new area; but lately, it doesn't seem to be acting according to it's programming.

Here we find our Adventurers dropping down onto this new world to uncover what has been ailing the area. Building off the original, players will leverage useful Items, discover new clues and defeat the Boss Encounter to win.

				
				
					Setup:The setup is very similar to the Classic Solitaire with the seven piles ranging from 1 to 7 in front of you forming the Dungeon and the rest of the cards in the Explore (or Hand) pile. These will be introduced into the game by flipping up three at a time. The key difference in the setup is that the Boss Encounter will start face up in the first pile of the play area, and the Starting Item will sit above it forming your inventory.

				
				
					How it Works:Play will be centered around forming sequences with your four (4) Adventurers and leveling them up into the Party piles at the top. Leveling up your Adventurers will in turn help you complete different Encounters until you are strong enough to beat the Boss and win the game!

—description from the designer

boardgame: Cavern Shuffle: Lair of the Lich
description:
The Premise:Based on the classic Solitaire, Cavern Shuffle is a solo-player dungeon delver game where the player must level up their party in order to uncover the dark twists and turns of the dungeon. Using familiar mechanics, players will uncover useful Items, overcome Obstacles and defeat the Boss Encounter to win!

				
				
					The Setting:Far away from any light, evil grows. Festering in the blackness lurks a darkness that is forming. This version of Cavern Shuffle will meet the Lich King. Haunting the corridors of their decrepit cavern; raising the bodies of those who have come to slay them, to now fight for them.

Here we find that our Adventurers’ have uncovered a cursed artefact that belongs to a Lich that has lingered too long. It is up to the party to delve into the darkness, to destroy this power before it grows too big.

				
				
					Setup:The setup is very similar to the Classic Solitaire with the seven piles ranging from 1 to 7 in front of you forming the Dungeon and the rest of the cards in the Explore (or Hand) pile. These will be introduced into the game by flipping up three at a time. The key difference in the setup is that the Boss Encounter will start face up in the first pile of the Dungeon, and the Starting Item will sit above it forming your inventory.

				
				
					How it Works:Play will be centered around forming sequences with your four (4) Adventurers and leveling them up into the Party piles at the top. Leveling up your Adventurers will in turn help you complete different Encounters in the Dungeon until you are strong enough to beat the Boss and win the game!

—description from the designer

Classical Risk game with World of Tanks flavour. Upgrade your tanks, conquer command bases and harbours while defending your HQ. Earn silver to buy upgrades and missions. Earn more victory points then your rivals before the "War is over" card is drawn, which happens with an increased probability after a certain amount of rounds.

In the bustling Italian city-states of the early 1500s, art was not only beauty but power. Workshops competed fiercely for prestige, fueled by the rivalry of great masters like Leonardo da Vinci and Michelangelo Buonarroti. Artists labored tirelessly to create masterpieces—from sweeping frescoes to marble statues—each work a statement of skill and vision.

These creations were carried across courts and palaces, traveling the Italian peninsula to be exhibited in galleries and public spaces, where they dazzled the eyes of nobles and citizens alike. But art alone was not enough. Success depended on securing the favor of the wealthy and powerful. Through charm, reputation, and spectacle, workshops sought to influence Collectors, drawing them to their galleries and winning their lifelong support.

In Maestro Michelangelo and da Vinci’s workshops compete for fame and aristocratic attention in various courts and galleries across the Italian peninsula. During the game, the two workshops are either represented by a single player or a team of players.

Players take turns resolving their actions, creating Artworks, exhibiting their art across the northern provinces of the Italian peninsula, influencing the taste of the powerful Collectors and competing for publicity of the local elite. The workshop with the most victory points after 4 rounds becomes a winner and is declared the most iconic artist of the Renaissance!

—description from the publisher
family: Theme: Fine Art and Art Museums

description:
Simple GBACW is an alternative to the Series Rules of GMT’s acclaimed Great Battles of the American Civil War series. Now players can use the GBACW maps and counters to recreate Civil War battles with a shorter, 16-page rulebook, allowing them to play even the largest scenarios to conclusion in a fraction of the time. The rules retain the feel of linear combat in the Civil War while shifting the focus from the individual regiments to the brigades as a whole. Many of the concepts found in the Simple Version are similar to players familiar with GBACW, but the Simple Version is not necessarily intended as a gateway or a stepping stone to the Series Rules.

Currently, the Simple Version rules are only available for 3 Days of Gettysburg – Deluxe Edition, but plans are in place to provide Simple Version Battle Books for both existing and upcoming games.
family: Series: Great Battles of the American Civil War (Richard H. Berg)

description:
In this simple roll-and-check game, you will try to serve the best Belgian frites, pushing your luck with a combination of crisp levels and frite sauces. Try to score a 6 from your dice to gain a new die, but be careful, once the dice pool runs out the game ends!

—description from the designer

boardgame: Chinese Dragons: The Pachanga Vip of Race Stars
description:
Chinese Dragons: The Pachanga Vip of Race Stars is a modern, UPV-enhanced reimagining of the classic star-shaped race game, now powered by layered play, chaos effects and tactical interaction.

At its core, the game uses the UPV System, a design framework that works in two layers:

• Layer 0 – The Chaos Layer:
Before each turn, the player must either spend a Token to “skip chaos” or roll the Blue Die.
The Blue Die can grant helpful boosts, mild challenges, or small twists that instantly change the plan. This keeps every round unpredictable, social and full of energy.

• Layer 1 – The Classic Strategy Layer:
After resolving the Blue Die, the game becomes a clean, familiar race: jump, chain-hop, advance toward your target triangle and outmaneuver opponents.

But the classic flow is constantly shaped by two tools:

• The Red Die (Chaos Direction Die):
Triggers table-wide effects such as: reverse turn order, force a piece backward, make all players move again, skip a turn, or trigger mini-events.
(Not all effects appear in every edition; they rotate by variant.)

• VSD Cards (Variable Star Deck):
These are the heart of UPV: each card has (1) a race challenge and (2) a score value.
A challenge might instruct the active player to hop in a specific direction, block an opponent, mirror a move, launch a chain-jump, swap a piece, or resolve a table interaction.
Players keep the card afterward for scoring.

Throughout the game, players choose between safety (spending Tokens to avoid chaos) or embracing the unpredictable path of the Blue Die.
Because chaos can harm you or help you spectacularly, every turn becomes a meaningful, emotional decision.

The result is a star-race experience that feels familiar but transforms into something cinematic: bold moves, reversals, reactions, tension, laughter and tactical surprise.

Chinese Dragons keeps the universal appeal of the original race-star format—simple, elegant and global—while adding enough dynamism and modularity to sustain endless variants and future expansions.

—description from the designer

A game of competitive balance. Players attempt to knock each other over by pushing or pulling until the opponent falls either off the back or into the middle of the balance track. The players both have the same four cards each turn: Push, Pull, Rise, and Sink; they simultaneously choose a card and use a rock-paper-scissors style hierarchy rondel to determine the result. Player pieces move based on the interaction.

When one player falls, the other player receives a point. Both players return their pieces to the "center" spots on the balance track and play resumes.

The first player to reach five points wins the game.

—description from the designer

The Palace of Versailles welcomes you to a historical escape game to play with family or friends!

Discover three independent scenarios that will immerse you in some of the most significant moments in French history:

     Poisons and Secrets, based on the Affair of the Poisons
     The Spy with a Hundred Faces, based on the story of the Chevalier d'Éon
     A Necklace of Deception, based on the Affair of the Queen's Necklace


The different scenarios can be solved in 1 hour and 15 minutes, with or without a game master. 
Read the scenarios, reveal the puzzle and clue cards, and solve the mysteries that await you.

Two players program two simultaneous games of Rock Paper Scissors and negotiate, bluff, and deduce their way to claiming prize cards which could lead them to riches or ruin within the classic cyclical hierarchy of Rock Paper Scissors. Since only the top card of each prize card stack is visible, there’s just enough info to set the stakes and get the wheeling and dealing started.

Ties open up a Pandora’s Box of interactive tiebreaking that could sink players deeper into devastation or lift them all the way out of the pit. Or maybe they could agree to just flip a coin and leave it up to the purest chance.

After six brief rounds, players calculate the value of their prize card piles and—even if their totals are negative—the player with the most points wins!

—description from the designer

Recap on the Basics

Cribbage is a classic point-based card game. Each player cuts the deck for deal; low card wins. In a two-player game, each player throws 2 of their 6 cards into the dealer’s “Crib”. The player who didn’t deal cuts the deck, and the dealer draws the “Cut Card”. This is the card that is shared between both players to create points with the players’ hands.

The next step in regular cribbage is the pegging round, where players use their cards to add up to 31. For example: Player one puts down a 10, and Player two puts down a 9, and so on. This round ends when all players have used all of their cards.

The player who did not deal counts their hand first. After this, the dealer counts their hand and crib, and the round ends.

The process repeats (with each player alternating dealer) until someone reaches 121 points and the game ends.

Cribbagio Mechanics

Cribbagio starts the same as cribbage, except each player draws a Peg Class card. This card is used for the duration of the game and gives the player additional ways to make points in both the pegging and counting rounds.

Whenever a player/team reaches (or is sent back to) 30, 60, and 90 points on the cribbage board, they draw one curse and one buff card. These Curse and Buff cards can only be played once a round: right after the cut card has been revealed and right before the “Pegging Phase” begins. Curse and Buff Cards are played in succession, and the dealer gets to play theirs last.

Peg Class Cards - Give players additional points for the duration of the game. For example, drawing the “Flush” peg class card will give that player the ability to make a flush out of three cards instead of four, even in your crib.
Curse Cards - Curse Cards are cards that are only used once per game and are drawn at 30, 60, and 90 points. These cards are used to limit the number of points and/or cards your opponent will have that round. For Example, One Curse Card called “Smackdown” makes it so that the opponent gets zero points from their crib that round. Players can stack Curse Cards to create insane card combos.
Buff Cards - Buff Cards are cards that are only used once a round and are drawn at 30, 60, and 90 points. These cards are meant to increase the points and/or cards that you get. For Example, one Buff Card is “Captain Peg Legs”, which doubles the points you receive from pegging during that round. Players can also stack Buff Cards to create insane card combos.

—description from the designer

boardgame: Кольт Экспресс: Юбилейное издание (Colt Express: 10th Anniversary)
description:
Кольт Экспресс: Юбилейное издание ("Colt Express: 10th Anniversary") is the base game with Happy 10th Anniversary expansion in one box.

It brings original content and exclusive, high-quality game elements, such as wooden screen-printed pawns and tokens, as well as two new bandits to play with even more players.
family: Players: Games with expansions that change player count

boardgame: Flashy: The Pachanga of Memory VIP
description:
Flashy: The Pachanga of Memory VIP reinvents the classic memory game through the Universal Pachanga Vip (UPV). Each turn begins in Layer 0: players must choose between spending a Token to stay safe, or rolling the Blue die to enter a high-risk, high-reward state.

If granted access to Layer 1, the player draws a VSD (Variable Scoring Deck) card that defines two things:

     the scoring value of any matching pair found this turn, and
     a temporary twist, restriction, bonus, or challenge applied during tile flipping.


Some Blue-die outcomes may instead trigger the Red die, adding chaotic effects such as turn swaps, tile disruptions, card steals, or forced flips. (Exact die faces are revealed only in the rulebook.)

Once in Layer 1, the player performs the classic “flip two tiles” action — now enhanced by UPV dynamics. If they succeed, the VSD card grants their score; if they fail, all temporary effects reset and the turn ends.

Flashy retains the simplicity and accessibility of traditional memory games, but adds tension, timing, tactical decisions, social interaction, and powerful replayability. Optional variants allow using d8 or d12 Blue dice for expanded challenge levels

—description from the designer

In this game, you first build your hand in real time, and then later play either Monopoly or Trick-Taking with the hand you've built.

During the first half of the hand-building phase, players take cards one by one from the face-down center of the table in real time, adding them to their hand if necessary, or returning them face-down to the center if not. When a player adds a STOP card to their hand, they declare STOP and temporarily halt the collection of cards. Each player checks their collected cards and votes using a voting card whether they want to play Monopoly or trick-taking. The voting card is then collected and one card is drawn at random, and the second half of the game phase is played according to the written rules. The hand-building phase resumes, and players continue collecting cards until one player has 13 cards. Players who have not reached 13 cards can continue to collect cards face-down to make up to 13 cards.

The criteria for collecting cards in your hand are set so that in Daifugo you want to get rid of all your cards, so you want to collect large numbers, while in Trick-Taking you lose points if you win, so you want to collect as small numbers as possible.

Two types of special cards also add a touch of difficulty to the players.

boardgame: Sharpy: The Pachanga of the # VIP
description:
Sharpy transforms the classic "#" grid game into a chaotic, strategic party battle using the UPV system (Layer-0 & Layer-1).
Players start each turn choosing to spend a Token (safe play) or roll the Blue Die (boosts, modifiers, or forced chaos).
Some Blue results trigger the Red Die, introducing sabotage, swaps, reversed turns, blocking cells, double placements and more.

Every line you form (horizontal, vertical, diagonal, or extended-board patterns) earns points through the VSD deck (Variable Scoring Deck), adding unpredictability and tactical planning.

Sharpy supports optional D8 and D12 dice variants for expanded chaos and long-term replay.

A fast, modern, highly social take on a timeless symbol: #.

—description from the designer

Is there something suspicious about this person's expression? A game where everyone can take commemorative photos.

Now, take a snap of everyone's best expressions with your smartphone! But wait, there's one ghost with a strange expression hiding in the crowd...! "Isn't there something strange about this person's face?" "No, you're the one who's suspicious!" Use everyone's opinions and discussions to expose the mischievous ghost! Maybe there's a psychic who knows the ghost's true identity...?

"It's a Ghost" is a hidden identity party game in which everyone takes a commemorative photo with their smartphones, then discusses and votes to reveal the identity of the lone ghost using a designated facial expression as a clue.

Players who successfully guess the ghost receive points!
However, if everyone correctly identifies the ghost, all players receive 1 point. However, if the votes are split, the ghost and those who voted for him receive points equal to the number of votes cast. The ghost's plan is to guide the discussion so that the votes are split appropriately.

Furthermore, this game isn't just about revealing the ghost; after voting, everyone, including the ghost, votes again for the person with the most attractive facial expression as the MVP!
The player with the most votes receives points equal to the number of votes cast, giving players a chance to score big!
Because the MVP point is significant, everyone must put their all into making the best facial expressions they can!

Adding a "medium," the only person who knows the ghost's existence, as an additional rule, can make the discussion even more lively!

"I'm a Ghost" is a party game where even the most innocent expressions somehow turn into hilarious laughs.
It's a game that kills two birds with one stone, allowing you to enjoy the photos you take as memories after playing.

The components are pretty luxurious.

10 card micro game of bluffing and set collection.

The game itself is a card pick where the one with the higher total wins, but if Piradero comes out, the lower total wins—it's a simple psychological battle with luck elements.

boardgame: Xurpy: The Pachanga of the Chinese Sticks VIP
description:
Xurpy is a dexterity-based race where players try to remove colored sticks without disturbing the rest — but now enhanced with the UPV (Universal Pachanga VIP) system.

At the beginning of your turn, you choose:

Spend a Token to play safely (classic mode), or

Roll the Blue Die, activating a random skill challenge, bonus, or restriction.

If the Blue Die triggers chaos, you roll the Red Die, which adds unpredictable social effects: reverse turn order, force swaps, steal sticks, temporary handicaps, and more.

Every successfully removed stick earns a card from the VSD (Variable Scoring Deck), giving each stick a different value, combo or conditional bonus.

Xurpy mixes dexterity, risk-management, chaotic interaction and strategic resource use. Fast, portable, highly replayable, and expandable with future themed VSD decks and optional d8/d12 dice variants.

—description from the publisher

boardgame: Brandenburger's in the Desert: Operation Condor 1942
description:
Operation Condor 1942 is set in North Africa 1942 before the "Battle of El Alamein 1942". 
It revolves around an historical German "Brandenburger" unit who crossed the desert to get behind British/Commonwealth lines. 
The five scenarios are "What If's". 
Because before the operation even started there was a fly in the ointment, his name "Himmler" head of the "SS". 
Had the operation succeeded, then the "Battle of El Alamein" could easily have had a different result, read and see why. 
Historical and scenario maps are included as well as photographs.

Scenarios are mainly set for scales from 6mm - 1/72nd, skirmish except the last which is the reenactment of the whole "Battle of El Alamein" October 1942 but with a BIG difference. 
This is best suited to a club, mega-game or board game.

Herond Quest is an extremely fast-paced card game for 2 to 6 players. The setting is Netvaria, a captivating world where ancient magic and forgotten technology fuse together.

You step into the role of a powerful competitor known as a Browser. Choose from 6 different Browsers, each with their own unique abilities, and compete in the renowned Herond Quest Tournament, striving to be the ultimate survivor.

Every turn is a pivotal sequence of decisions:


     Draw Cards: Gain resources for the fight ahead.
     Take a Main Action: Choose your strategy. You can cast powerful spells, gather more mana, or take a high-risk, high-reward chance by using the dangerous Curse Magic Deck. With over 20 unique spells, you can build stunning combos to outplay your rivals!
     Check for Elimination: At the end of your turn, count your accumulated Curses.


The catch is simple but brutal: The stronger you grow, the more Curses you accumulate. While you can "Awaken" your Browser for extra power, doing so brings you perilously closer to elimination.

The core rule is a thrilling balance between power and danger: If you collect too many Curses, you are instantly knocked out! The game ends when only one person remains victorious.

Herond Quest also includes varied game modes like intense Team Battles and cooperative Co-op Play, ensuring plenty of opportunities to experience the push-your-luck tension, explosive card interactions, and fast, highly replayable battles.

—description from the publisher

Wargaming with Panzerjagers and Sturmgeschutz is the first in a series volumes dealing specifically with histories of units and scenarios. 
This first volume deals with the "Mystery Waffens SS Panzer Division that never existed. 
The era is April 1945 location the German city of Hannover. 
The main combatants being the U.S. 84th Infanty Division, with attached Tank Destroyer and Independent Tank Battalions, versus Kampfgruppe Nicolussi Leck, backed up by the Internal Flak Division/Regiment of Hannover and a handful of German infantry, Hitler Youth and Volksturm units.

This volume includes accurate T.o.E.'s for the units involved, 6 coloured scenario maps (with two additional possible scenarios mentioned), photographs of many of the main weapons and maps showing locations of towns, villages, rivers and more.

See if you can manage to do what Nicolussi-Leck managed to do with a handful of Panzerjagers (Jagdpanthers) and armoured carriers (Sd.Kfz 250/251's). This unit became know as the "Mystery Waffen SS Panzer Division".

Though only 26 pages long, these are packed with accurate history, T.o.E.'s and games for those into wargaming with a difference.

Monopoly: Ted Lasso celebrates the critically acclaimed, Emmy Award-winning comedy series where fans can believe in Believe! Buy, sell, and trade properties like Nelson Road Stadium, The Owner’s Box, and Keeley’s Flat, while advancing or gaining riches with Lasso-inspired Believe (Community Chest) cards and Smells Like Potential (Chance) cards. Customized tokens include some of the most memorable symbols from the show such as Ted’s visor, a goldfish (aka the happiest animal in the world), a box of those famous little biscuits, a teacup, toy army man and everyone's favorite 'Believe' sign.
A fast-paced dexterity micro football game where players flick, position, and outsmart their opponent in a tight, high-interaction tabletop arena.

London, 1889: A burglary has been committed at a lord's home on the famous Baker Street! 
Sherlock Holmes is too busy, so he asks you to investigate in his place... 
Interrogate the suspects and unmask the culprit before his return. 
In this adventure, you'll need to observe, manipulate objects, and solve puzzles just like in a real escape room to crack this mystery in 60 minutes or less!

—description from the publisher

A tactical game for up to 6 players.

All players simultaneously choose a piece, which they place face down in the middle of the table. Once everyone has chosen, the pieces are revealed.

Points are awarded (and recorded): 

     Types that appear only once: 1 point
     Types that appear 3 times: 2 points
     Types that appear 4 or more times: 3 points


All other pieces receive no points, especially those that appear twice.

Now the tiles on the table are sorted by type and grouped together so that everyone can see them clearly. The players then choose another tile according to the above pattern, which is also scored. This is done 6 times until all the tiles are in the middle of the table.

End of round/game:
The player with the most points wins. Any number of rounds can be played.

—description from the publisher

A tactical game for 2-5 players, with the aim of rolling the highest possible scores for your own (secret) rabbits.

The starting player rolls all the dice, then takes one of the dice and places it on a free rabbit figure. Then he rolls the remaining dice and takes another dice, which he places on another rabbit. This is repeated 4 times until each rabbit has a die.

Scoring:
The points for the rabbits are noted on a piece of paper (e.g. red 3, yellow 1, green 2, blue 0) – the rabbit means no points. Then the next player takes their turn in clockwise order.
The aim is not to reveal your colour too early by scoring too many points, so that the other players do not completely negate that colour.

End of the game:
The game ends when one rabbit colour has reached at least 50 points.

—description from the publisher (translated)

A tactical game for two or more players, with the aim of hurling the remains of the former red giant further and further away from the white dwarf.
One player begins. They select a red wooden cylinder and measure its distance from the White Dwarf in the centre. They must now use this distance to move another red wooden cylinder further away from the White Dwarf. This does not have to be in a straight line, but the stone must be further away from the White Dwarf afterwards.
The players take turns in clockwise order.

End of round/game:
If a player cannot move any of the red cylinders further away from the white dwarf, they are eliminated. They move the white cylinder and five red cylinders as they wish so that the remaining players can start again. As soon as only one player remains, they have won the game.

—description from the publisher

The aim is to find matching star clusters. To do this, the player turns over one cluster and then another. If they find a match, they can keep it (1 point) or continue searching. Collecting three matching clusters is worth 5 points, four matching clusters are worth 10 points.

However, whenever the player reveals an incorrect cluster, they must turn ALL open clusters back over.

For each pair of clusters or each set of three or four, the player may now also search for matching tiles among the raw materials. And this happens every round. So if they have two pairs and a triple, they may take the following step three times in that round (and the following rounds).

The star shown above indicates what is being searched for. The player must now reveal matching resource counters of this type. They may turn over any two counters.

—description from the publisher

This is a game themed around alcohol-loving dinosaurs gathering in a saloon to drink.

When two players clash, an early action occurs! So, the faster the player, the better.

Play cards from your hand trying not to match other players. However if you do be the first to draw your gun card or lose a health point. Lose three health points and your eliminated.

Each player takes on the role of a museum curator. Their goal is to achieve the highest possible prestige. To do so, they must navigate the world of fine arts and acquire artworks to expand their museum’s collection.

The game is played on a board featuring an outer track, similar to a Monopoly-style circuit. By rolling a die, players move their tokens along this track and carry out—or endure—the events indicated on each space (exhibitions, artwork purchases, trips abroad, etc.).
At the center of the board is a spiral that tracks each player’s notoriety points: players advance their marker along this notoriety scale as the game’s events allow them to gain (or fail to gain) points. The first player to reach the top of the scale wins.

The game components also include:

     A deck of 78 cards featuring a selection of artworks, divided into six categories—sculpture, objects, landscapes, portraits, religion, and genre scenes (13 cards each).
     A deck of 52 quiz cards containing the questions and answers corresponding to the matching spaces on the board.
family: Theme: Fine Art and Art Museums

description:
Collect 3 bees and win the game!
Each player starts with 5 cards, and the multicolor bee is placed in the center along with the decks. On your turn, place a card next to the hive without exceeding the value of the central bee. If it matches the color of another card, remove it! Complete the hive without going over and the bee is yours. If you can’t play, the last person who placed a card takes the bee. If you run out of cards, the bee escapes and no one scores. At the end of each round, refill your hand and reveal a new bee. Keep playing until someone collects 3 bees and becomes the champion!

—description from the publisher

Le jeu de Routard ("The Routard Game") is a board game for 2 to 4 players aged 8 and up, centered on travel and exploration. In the first phase, each player prepares their backpack: they roll a die to earn money, which they can spend on administrative documents, travel guides, equipment, plane tickets, hotel vouchers, and more.

Once equipped, players move on to the second phase: the journey itself. They advance across a board featuring various airports, pay for their travels, and draw “Accommodation” cards or interact with the effects of the spaces they land on—whether it’s buying items, going on adventures, or completing reports. Some spaces trigger special events (“Job,” “Chance,” “Poste restante”), and players can help each other by hitchhiking or rely on “Routard Assistance” when needed. The winner is the first to reach the final airport while holding two “Purchase,” two “Adventure,” and two “Report” cards.

In Oniasobi, both players use the powers of oni (japanese demons) to reduce their opponent's life to 0, starting from 50.

Players alternate between the roles of attacker and defender every round, dueling by choosing from a few options which are revealed simultaneously then swapped.

In a basic attack and defense, each player draws an oni card, for which the value difference determines the attack.
Other effects may include life restoration, building a reserve, reversing the opponent's effect or using the power of a specific oni by sacrificing your life.
A Top Trumps rip-off themed around "adulting" problems characterized as monsters. Launched as part of a Halloween promotional campaign for soda brand Fanta in 2025 only in Brazil.

Welcome to the Senate of Arguments of Manipulation.

In this card game, you take on the role of a senator and use your abilities (called arguments) to help your faction win in the Senate.

Either you are a loyalist and want to crush the burgeoning rebellion in your ranks, or you are a rebel with the goal of forcing the corrupt loyalists to resign.

20 unique Roles:

     8x Rebel roles
     11x Loyalist roles
     1x Lobbyist
     62x Argument cards (Skills of a role)
     20x Filecards



     No Game Master needed
     You can continue playing even if you are voted out
     You can keep your eyes open during the game


"Werwolf for adults"

—description from the publisher

This instalment in the Suomen Historiapelit series takes us to the artist community of painters, sculptors, writers, composers and others that sprung up around lake Tuusulanjärvi (about 30 km north of Helsinki) in the early 20th century and included such household names as Jean Sibelius. While roll-and-moving around the lake and the various artists' villas, studios and other places of interest built around it, players will get to know those personalities and the history of the place.

Fifteenth game in the Nähdään! series of trivia games about Finnish cities and towns. By playing this game, you'll learn to know just about everything there is to know about the landmarks, historical events and personages of this small (but growing!) town straddling the Highway 4 (E75) about 60 kilometers northeast of Helsinki, and perhaps best known to most of the Finnish people elsewhere from the 1970's pop song 'Mäntsälä mielessäin' (Mäntsälä On My Mind)

Like the preceding few titles in the series (except for the 2nd version of Helsinki) this is a single-deck trivia game. This game with 312 questions has been designed in cooperation with the local homestead association, Mäntsälä-Seura Ry.

All aboard for a wild ride across the Wild West! Use your intelligence and bravery to defeat the dangers on board the Chocolate Express.

Escape the Chocolate Express is an exciting adventure where players have to cooperate, but also keep in mind their personal goal. Work together to stop the escaped train, but try to collect as much chocolate as you can along the way.

Includes 32 chocolate game pieces.

boardgame: インゼルシーカー (Insel Seeker)
description:
Insel Seeker is a middle-heavyweight game of 90-120 minutes of resource management by Drafting and Tableau Building.
Each player has their own island sheet, exploring diverse landscapes to gather resources and discover ruins.

In multiplayer games, interaction comes through drafting and comparing the number of buildings constructed. In solo play, you can fully immerse yourself in the adventure of your own island.

—description from the publisher

A ferocious beast dwells in the castle!

Or, at least, that’s what the misinformed villagers believe. You know better, though, seeing as how you live in the castle. The cursed lord who lives in this isolated citadel is in danger, and you’ll have to mount a fierce defense from all who now march on the Beast’s castle. It’s up to you to defend the Tanglewoods!

The only dark menace in the forest is the hearts of those who wish harm upon the peaceful occupants of the castle. Convince them to leave this hostile path and return to their villages. It’s time to put all of that scholarly reading to work and show them what you’re made of.

From the mind of Manny Trembley, Tanglewoods: Rose is a new take on a familiar world, debuting in the 20 Strong game system and featuring his exquisite art. Players will select a hero and defeat the enemies attacking the Beast’s castle before time runs out. Ushering the foolish attackers into the Rose Garden will ensure the safety of the cursed lord.

Pizza Faction is an irreverent, interaction-packed deck-builder where players compete to be the first pizza parlor to earn $20 in tips. Expect strategic deck building, playful sabotage, and boisterous throwdowns to fight other players for their hard earned tips. With its humorous tone and take-that mechanics, Pizza Faction brings lively competition and nonstop laughter to the table.

—description from the publisher

Four ghosts living in an abandoned castle have invited colleagues for a grand party that's supposed to last all night. Alas, four intrepid children have decided to explore the castle, an intrusion that threatens to spoil the festivities. Each player must move their standy ghost to the different rooms of the castle. Tokens have been placed randomly and face down in each room. On their turn, the ghost reveals one token. If the token represents a ghost guest, the player rolls the fear die. On a "not scared" face, the guest leaves the castle; on a "scared" face, the token is returned face down. If the token represents an object, a bat, or a spider, the player must perform the corresponding mime as indicated by the game rules. When he fails, he will not play in the next round. If the token represents a child, the player rolls the fear die. On a "scared" face, the intruder leaves the castle; on a "not scared" face, the token is returned face down. The game ends when all four kids have left (game won) or when all four guest ghosts have left (game lost). The key to success is remembering the locations of both the guest ghosts and the kids.

Solus: Lost Second Edition is a tense solo sci-fi survival card game where players must Navigate through an unknown galaxy filled with danger. Fight enemy ships, make difficult decisions, and activate the required homelinks to find your way out.

With improved components, refined mechanics, and expanded strategic choices, this second edition heightens the challenge and immersion. Atmospheric, strategic, and deeply replayable, Solus: Lost delivers a gripping solo experience that rewards those who remain cool under pressure.

—description from the designer

Based on U&P Games' W1815, Austerlitz, 1805 takes the system to a new level.

The game is playable by one, two or four players (arguably by 0 since the innovative solitaire system can be used to drive both sides). The small number of rules means the game is quick to learn or to teach and it is equally quick to play (typically 20-40 minutes).

Optional Tactics Cards extend the game and introduce additional historical flavour and uncertainty - enhancing the already excellent replayability.

The game comes with:

A 600mm x 600 mounted map
A dual-layer admin board
36 wooden formation blocks
4 wooden admin blocks
8 counters
12 formation cards
27 tactics cards

There will also be a 'meeple' based edition.

—description from the publisher
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

description:
PrismFall is a fast, color-driven card game of pressure, timing, and table awareness for 2–6 players. Designed to be instantly learnable yet strategically engaging, the game blends family-friendly accessibility with tense multiplayer decision-making.

Instead of health or combat elimination, PrismFall uses a unique Coloruption™ system. Players accumulate Coloruption as they are targeted by card effects and combos. When a player reaches 20 Coloruption, their spectrum is on the brink of collapse—further pressure forces them to discard cards to survive. If they can no longer absorb the excess, they are eliminated.

The game is played with a single shared community deck, ensuring no deck construction, no power imbalance, and minimal setup. On each turn, players:

Draw one card
Play either a single card, a multi-card Chroma String (2–4 card combo), or pass
Place one card face-down into a Reserve, which serves as a tactical buffer and bluffing tool

Chroma Strings are the core of the game’s combo system. Playing multiple cards together replaces individual effects with stronger, predefined actions, rewarding timing and hand management without requiring complex rules or keywords.

To prevent early dog-piling, PrismFall includes a Focus-Fire Defense rule: if multiple opponents pressure the same player in a round, their Reserve absorbs damage at increased strength, keeping the game dynamic and social.

Games typically last 5–12 minutes, making PrismFall ideal for families, casual groups, warm-ups, or repeat play. With no player death, no violent themes, and a strong emphasis on momentum and collapse, PrismFall offers a fresh take on fast-play multiplayer card games.

PrismFall launches with a fully locked Version 1.0 ruleset. The design intentionally supports future expansion modules and advanced play variants, which build on the same core mechanics without altering the base game.

—description from the publisher

— Designer’s Note —

Design Note from the Creator

PrismFall v1.0 is intentionally locked.

What you’re seeing on this page represents the complete ruleset and design philosophy for the first era of the game. We’re not holding mechanics back, and we’re not planning mid-campaign revisions.

Future expansions already exist at the design level — but they are separate eras, not patches to v1.

When PrismFall grows, it will do so through explicit, opt-in modules, not rule creep or forced upgrades.

In short:

v1 is complete
v1 will stand on its own
future content will respect that boundary

We believe games should be finished when they ship — and allowed to earn history afterward.

boardgame: Defenders of the Wild: Critter Moon
description:
As the machine occupation tightens its choke hold on the Commonwood, a new moon rises. Critters from far and wide have joined the Defenders to strike a blow against the invasion that could change the course of the war! Join the Critter Moon Rebellion, join the Defenders of the Wild!

The Critter Moon expansion for the cooperative board game of animals against machines, Defenders of the Wild, features 8 new Critter Moon organizer cards, 2 per faction, each with it’s own unique play style and personality; 48 new defender cards, 6 per organizer; 8 new printed wooden animal organizers; Critter Moon Rebellion pamphlet and more.

—description from the publisher

Sumigi is a fast, strategic card game that turns addition into an exciting race of logic, memory, and tactical disruption. Players work with blue Addend cards and red Sum cards. On each turn, they decide whether to declare a valid mathematical set, disrupt an opponent with an attack card, or draw to strengthen their hand. The core of the game revolves around building combinations of addends whose total matches the value of one of the player’s open red cards.

Declaring a set requires both accuracy and clarity: players must show their Addend cards and verbally state the correct calculation. A successful declaration closes one of their red Sum cards; an incorrect declaration discards the attempt with no progress. Special cards such as “Exchange,” “-2 blues,” “+1 red,” “Stop,” “Bounce,” and the wild “Sumigi” card introduce interaction and sabotage, creating shifting opportunities for clever timing and defense.

Sumigi blends hand management, pattern recognition, and tactical card play. Players must evaluate probability, manage risk, and track opponents’ positions while identifying efficient additive combinations. Whether played casually or competitively, the game rewards flexibility, math intuition, and quick decision-making.

The goal is simple: be the first player to close all your red Sum cards. With multiple modes of play—including team, scoring, match, and elimination variants—Sumigi offers a blend of educational value and engaging strategy that scales across ages and experience levels.

—description from the publisher

Multigi is a fast, strategic card game that turns multiplication into an exciting race of logic, memory, and tactical disruption. Players work with blue Factor cards and red Product cards. On each turn, they decide whether to declare a valid mathematical set, disrupt an opponent with an attack card, or draw to strengthen their hand. The core of the game revolves around building combinations of factors whose product matches the value of one of the player’s open red cards.

Declaring a set requires both accuracy and clarity: players must show their Factor cards and verbally state the correct calculation. A successful declaration closes one of their red Product cards; an incorrect declaration discards the attempt with no progress. Special cards such as “Exchange,” “-2 blues,” “+1 red,” “Stop,” “Bounce,” the wild “Multigi” card, and the “Square” card introduce interaction and sabotage, creating shifting opportunities for clever timing and defense.

Multigi blends hand management, pattern recognition, and tactical card play. Players must evaluate probability, manage risk, and track opponents’ positions while identifying efficient multiplicative combinations. Whether played casually or competitively, the game rewards flexibility, math intuition, and quick decision-making.

The goal is simple: be the first player to close all your red Product cards. With multiple modes of play—including team, scoring, match, and elimination variants—Multigi offers a blend of educational value and engaging strategy that scales across ages and experience levels.

—description from the publisher

Faktorigi is a fast, strategic card game that turns prime factorization into an exciting race of logic, memory, and tactical disruption. Players work with blue Prime Number cards and red Product cards. On each turn, they decide whether to declare a valid mathematical set, disrupt an opponent with an attack card, or draw to strengthen their hand. The core of the game revolves around building combinations of prime factors whose product matches the value of one of the player’s open red cards.

Declaring a set requires both accuracy and clarity: players must show their Prime Number cards and verbally state the correct calculation. A successful declaration closes one of their red Product cards; an incorrect declaration discards the attempt with no progress. Special cards such as “Exchange,” “-2 blues,” “+1 red,” “Stop,” “Bounce,” the wild “Faktorigi” card, and the specialized “Square” and “Cube” cards introduce interaction and sabotage, creating shifting opportunities for clever timing and defense.

Faktorigi blends hand management, pattern recognition, and tactical card play. Players must evaluate probability, manage risk, and track opponents’ positions while identifying efficient prime-factor combinations. Whether played casually or competitively, the game rewards flexibility, math intuition, and quick decision-making.

The goal is simple: be the first player to close all your red Product cards. With multiple modes of play—including team, scoring, match, and elimination variants—Faktorigi offers a blend of educational value and engaging strategy that scales across ages and experience levels.

—description from the publisher

The Matematigi Collection is a fast, strategic trio of math card games that turns addition, multiplication, and prime factorization into an exciting race of logic, memory, and tactical disruption. Players engage with blue Addend, Factor, and Prime Number cards alongside red Sum and Product cards. On each turn, they decide whether to declare a valid mathematical set, disrupt an opponent with an attack card, or draw to strengthen their hand. Across all three games, the core experience revolves around building the right numerical combinations to match the values shown on a player’s open red cards.

Declaring a set requires both accuracy and clarity: players must show their cards and verbally state the correct calculation, whether forming sums, products, or prime-factor products. A successful declaration closes one of their red cards; an incorrect declaration discards the attempt with no progress. Special cards such as “Exchange,” “-2 blues,” “+1 red,” “Stop,” “Bounce,” and each game’s wild card—“Sumigi,” “Multigi,” or “Faktorigi”—shape interaction and sabotage, while additional tools like the “Square” and “Cube” cards appear in the more advanced games, creating shifting opportunities for clever timing and defense.

The Matematigi Collection blends hand management, pattern recognition, and tactical card play. Players must evaluate probability, manage risk, and track opponents’ positions while identifying efficient additive, multiplicative, or prime-factor combinations. Whether played casually or competitively, the collection rewards flexibility, math intuition, and quick decision-making, offering a progression of difficulty that grows with the player.

The goal in each game is simple: be the first player to close all your red cards. With multiple modes of play—including team, scoring, match, and elimination variants—Sumigi, Multigi, and Faktorigi together offer a blend of educational value and engaging strategy that scales across ages, experience levels, and mathematical readiness.

—description from the publisher

After decades of secretly weakening the native population of planet Bropusie through the emission of transdimensional waves, the alien race known as the Prezuknés finally initiates a full-scale invasion. Their goal is clear: to conquer the planet by destroying and replacing its inhabitants.

Using advanced infiltration and biological replacement technology, the Prezuknés begin penetrating multiple regions of the planet and its five moons. However, they failed to anticipate the awakening of a group of Bropusie warriors who resisted the alien manipulation. United by courage and ancestral spirit, these warriors rise to reclaim their homeland.

But time is short. If the Prezuknés succeed in replacing the Bropusie race, the entire planet will fall under their rule forever.

Objective

Each player represents one race and has a distinct mission:

Bropusie (Warriors):
Save the Bropusie race by defending, reclaiming, and conquering all mandatory Zones.

Prezuknés (Aliens):
Destroy and replace the Bropusie race by conquering all mandatory Zones and completing the invasion.

Target Audience

Primary audience: Teenagers and adults

Secondary audience: Families
Suitable for players who enjoy strategy, tactical challenges, and asymmetric gameplay.

Recommended configurations:

Balanced teams: 2 vs 2, 3 vs 3, etc.

If teams are unequal, the smaller team must start with the same number of "Zona" cards as the larger team.

Starting the Game

Each race begins with:


     At least 2 Zones
     20 tokens


A fixed number of Mandatory Zone Cards (cannot be reduced)

Play proceeds clockwise.

Zone Rules

A Zone is conquered when all its Nexuses are filled with tokens from one race.

A Zone is controlled when more than 50% of its Nexuses are filled by one race.

Each race's Mandatory Zones are the Zones they must conquer to win.

How Rounds Work

In each round, every race must perform the following actions (in any chosen order):

1. Roll the Dice

Determines the number of tokens that can be freely placed onto the board.

Exception: Moons cannot be reached with dice-based placement.
Moons require Power Cards for deployment.

2. Spin the Spinner

Determines:


     Number and type of resources gained
     Attributes available for purchase (Attack, Defense, Precision)


3. Game board Action
The player use their tokens, resources, attributes and/or power Cards to deploy strategic effects.

Winning the Game

The game ends when one race conquers all its Mandatory Zones.

If both races achieve their objectives simultaneously, or if a tie must be resolved, use the tiebreakers in this order:

Tiebreakers (in descending order):


     Most Mandatory Zones conquered
     Most total Zones controlled
     Most moons conquered
     Highest number of tokens placed across the whole map (planet + moons)


If there is still a tie, the two races must sign a peace treaty, ending the conflict.

This supplement continues to expand on the game, introducing a wealth of new rules to add even more variety, scope and excitement to your battles in the underhive and ash wastes.

Inside you will find:

     Ironhead Squat Prospectors: Updated rules for creating and running Ironhead Squat prospectors, including extensive weapon and Wargear lists, as well as two new Ironhead Squat fighters and the Svenotar Scout Trike.

This is a supplement. You will need a copy of the Necromunda: Core Rulebook to use the contents of this book.

The police called it suicide, and the world moved on—except for one person. Years later, her disgraced fiancé is reaching out for help, desperate to clear his name.

Could there be more to Danni’s death than anyone realized? It’s up to you to reopen the case and uncover the truth.

boardgame: Brothers In Arms: The Card Game
description:
Brothers in Arms is a compact, solo tactical wargame set during World War II, designed to be played entirely in-hand without requiring a table. Players command a squad of six British soldiers on a desperate mission in occupied Normandy, facing numerically superior enemy forces. The objective is to eliminate all enemy combatants before they destroy or disperse your squad. Featuring no dice, the game uses printed random values on cards to resolve all tests.

Theme & Setting

Isolated from all support, a six-man British squad advances through the ruins of occupied Normandy seeking a critical enemy outpost. Cut off from reinforcements and outnumbered, these soldiers must rely on tactics, teamwork, and courage to survive—or risk being annihilated or scattered by German forces.

Core Gameplay

The game uses a unified deck system where the same card deck serves as the Player's available actions, the Enemy's AI automaton, and the source of all randomness. Players manage six named soldiers with individual abilities, while the Enemy operates through an automated system. No dice are needed—all uncertainty is resolved through printed values on cards, with constant tension maintained by discarding the visible card before reading future results.

Victory & Defeat Conditions

     The Player wins by reducing the Enemy Level to 0.


The Player loses if:

     Five of six soldiers are killed
     The squad's two senior leaders are both killed
     The Enemy successfully escapes beyond maximum range while in Withdraw mode
     All remaining soldiers are suppressed and unable to act


Key Mechanics

Distance & Positioning

Distance ranges from 0 (melee) to 4 (maximum). The Player controls movement strategy, while the Enemy typically advances under normal conditions. Strategic tension emerges from choosing whether to engage at close range for powerful effects or maintain distance for tactical advantages.

Terrain & Combat

Terrain cards provide asymmetric attack and defense modifiers. Villages, hills, forests, bunkers, swamps, and open fields create meaningful positional choices. Squads in movement suffer penalties to both attack and defense, incentivizing tactical positioning.

Squad Management

Each soldier possesses unique abilities and can become suppressed, losing effectiveness until recovered. The tension between aggression and unit preservation drives decision-making throughout the game.

Mission Variants

Three narrative missions alter objectives and add replayability: recovering a downed soldier, capturing a strategic location, and infiltrating to retrieve intelligence. Each modifies setup, victory conditions, and core rules.

Why Play

Brothers in Arms appeals to players seeking:

     Tactical solo wargaming in a truly portable format
     Strategic decision-making through resource management
     Mechanical elegance and coherence
     Emergent narrative and high replayability
     Challenging gameplay where every choice matters


—description from the designer

boardgame: Swaysy: The Pachanga of Balance VIP
description:
Swaysy is a balancing dexterity game supercharged by the Universo Pachanga VIP (UPV) system: dice, tokens, and challenge cards that turn a simple physical puzzle into a storm of tension, laughter, and tactical decisions. At its core, Swaysy uses a central pivoting platform (like a see-saw) where players place and remove wooden blocks, trying to keep the structure in precarious equilibrium. Before each move, a player must decide: pay a limited VIP token to ignore the chaos and play “clean”… or roll the blue Pachanga die and accept whatever twist the system throws at their turn.

The blue die controls how you approach the balance challenge (extra risk, restrictions, small bonuses, or sometimes triggering the red die), while the red die unleashes direct chaos on the table: shifting the order of play, forcing extra movements, affecting specific zones of the structure, or pushing players into high-risk maneuvers. When a player successfully completes their challenge, they draw a Variable Scoring Deck (VSD) card that shows positive and/or negative points; cards where the challenge fails can still stay in front of the player as negative scoring. At the end of the game, each player sums all their positive VSD values and subtracts the negative ones to determine a clear winner, not just “the one who didn’t knock it over”.

Beyond the base game with standard d6 dice, Swaysy is designed as a sandbox for expansions: you can swap the blue and red dice for d8 or d12 versions to introduce more granular effects and difficulty levels, use different balancing shapes (single bar, cross-shaped arms, multi-arm star, circular ring), or even advanced editions with gently rotating platforms to add motion to the equilibrium puzzle. The same VSD concept supports many thematic decks (family, party, expert, seasonal, kids, etc.), each with its own mix of challenges and scoring profiles, while still using the same physical components.

Swaysy is not just one game, but a flexible system that sits on top of a pure balancing core: familiar and easy to teach, but endlessly remixable through dice faces, card sets, and platform layouts. The base rules remain simple—place and remove blocks without crashing the structure—but the UPV layer adds social table talk, push-your-luck decisions, and a constant sense that every turn might be the one that tips everything over in the most glorious way.

—description from the designer

Inside an elegant black matte box, you will find a detailed miniature and the cards to play it in:


     Black Rose Wars/Black Rose Wars: Rebirth as a Servant
     The Breach as a Bug
     Nova Aetas Renaissance as an Enemy


A unique, cross-era, collectible game piece.
Each box includes a Certificate of Authenticity, confirming the unique number of your limited-edition Promo Black Friday 2025.

—description from the publisher

The SYW Battle Series (SYWBS) games are playable grand-tactical studies of battles of the Seven Years War. The series’ intent is to show how command operates on the battlefields, using the weapons and tactics of the period. The system emphasizes command & control and playability.

The games all share the following characteristics:
Scale = 200-220 m/hex (219-240 yrd/hex)
Time = 30 min/Turn
Counter = 1 Brigade, 1 Battalion, 1 to 3 Squadrons, 1 Battery, 1 Leader
Each unit's historical flag and uniform are displayed on Counters, Orders/organizational Displays, and Orders of battle (in Playbooks).

Kolin 1757 simulates the battle fought on the 18th of June 1757 in Bohemia, near Prague, during the Seven Years War. F.M. Graf Leopold Daun commanded the Austrian army, with a contingent of cavalry from Saxony: 53,790 men (51 bns, 43 grenadier coys, 158 sqns) and 162 guns in total. King of Prussia Frederick II the Great commanded the Prussian army: 34,000 men (33 bns, 116 sqns) and 94 guns.

—description from the designer
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

boardgame: Obsession: Dice Tower Family Board Promo
description:
Originally available as part of A Pack from the Dice Tower 2025 Gamefound campaign.

The Dice Tower Promo is not a new family but an alternative family board to be played with an existing family. This promo is a board only and does not include card, tiles, markers, servants, etc for a family.

Thematically, your family has fallen in love with cards and novel games and, finding the parlour too small, you have constructed a gaming Tower at one end of your manor house.  now, there are multiple venues at your country estate and a stream of up-and-coming gentry--some desirable, some not so much --are always ready to sit to a game.

Momo – The Word-Battle Game is a fast, fun and addictive party card game for 2 to 6 players, ideal for friends or family gatherings — perfect for an aperitif, a weekend with pals, or a cozy holiday evening.

How to play :
Each round, every player flips over a card from their personal pile at the same time. The card imposes a linguistic challenge: maybe one or several letters are mandatory, another letter is forbidden, or there’s a theme to respect.
Then you race to shout out a valid word that meets the constraints. The fastest to name a correct word wins the round — but if you hesitate or give an invalid word, you lose.

If you fail (or are last), you collect all the cards from that round. When all piles are empty, the player with the fewest cards collected wins the game.

—description from the publisher (translated)

Momo La Bataille des Mots, le jeu parfait pour faire travailler les méninges tout en s’amusant !
